question: Which one of the following has been named the National Aquatic Animal of India?
explanation: The Ganges river Dolphin or also called “Susu” is the National Aquatic Animal of India. • The Ganges River Dolphins (Platanista Gangetica) was officially discovered in 1801. • National Aquatic Animal of India is found in Ganges-Brahmaputra- Meghna River System. • Scientific name is Platanista Gangetica. Gangetic dolphin is a critically endangered species in India and therefore; has been included in Schedule I of the wildlife (protection) Act, 1972. • It represents the purity of the river Ganga as it can only survive in pure and fresh water. The Ministry of Environment and Forests notified the Ganges River Dolphin as the National Aquatic Animal on 18th May 2010.

question: Given below are the two statements, labelled Assertion (A) and Reason (R). options.a: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
options.b: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

explanation: By virtue of its location and stupendous height, the Great Himalaya Range obstructs the passage of cold continental air from the north into India in winter. The magnanimous Himalayas is still growing and the mighty Aravali range standing witness to the tides of time. However it is the youngest and the oldest mountains in the world. So both option given are correct but they are not co- related to each other. Hence reason is not the correct statement of assertion.

question: The world's highest road from Manali to Leh has been constructed at an average height of
explanation: The Leh - Manali Highway is 428 km long highway in Northernmost India connecting Leh to Manali (Himachal Pradesh). The Average elevation of Leh-Manali Highway is 4000m and its highest elevation is 5328m at the Taglang La Mountain pass (Ladakh). • The Leh-Manali Highway, which acts as a trunk route for various India-China Border Road.

question: Between which mountain ranges does "Leh" lie?
explanation: Leh is a city located in Leh district of Ladakh State, India. It is the district headquarter. It is situated between the Zaskar and Ladakh mountain ranges. Highways from Leh to Srinagar Kullu Valley and Karakoram Pass. National Highway NH(1D) connects it to Srinagar in the northwest. It has the highest Seasonal Observatory in Asia. question: The Naga Hills form the watershed between India and
explanation: The Naga Hills forms the watershed between Myanmar and India. The Naga hills, reaching a height of around 3,825 meters (12,549 ft) lie on the border of India and Burma (Myanmar). question: Which of the following is the oldest part of India?

question: Approximately how much is the net area under crops sown in India?
explanation: India's total geographic area is 329 million (32.9 crore) hectares. Out of this 195 million (19.5 crore) hectare is gross cropped area & 141 million (14.1 crore) hectare is net sown area. On the other hand, net irrigated area is only 65.3 million (6.53 crore) hectare. Rest of land is rainfed.

question: World environment day is celebrated on :
explanation: World Environment day is celebrated annually on 5 June. This day raises awareness about the environmental issues like global warming, marine pollution, human over-population, protection of wildlife and sustainable consumption. The theme of 2021 Environment Day is " Ecosystem Restoration".

question: Which one of the following experiences the least annual range of temperature?
explanation: On Equator the duration of the day or night is the same in equatorial countries. The sun's ray fall vertical all throughout the year. Hence, there is no worthwhile difference between summer and winter seasons. This is the reason that the lowest annual temperature range is found in equatorial areas.

question: Indus Basin 127. Which of the following rivers does NOT originate in India?
explanation: Sutlej– It rises from the Manasarovar- Rakshatal Lakes in Western Tibet. • Before Entering the Punjab plain, it cuts a gorge in Naina Devi Dhar where the famous Bhakra Dam has been constructed. • It is the largest of the five tributaries of the Indus River. • It eventually drains into the Indus in Pakistan. • Chenab– It is formed by confluence of two rivers, Chandra and Bhaga. • It originates from Lahaul Valley in Himachal Pradesh. • It flows through the Jammu region of Jammu and Kashmir into the plains of Punjab, Pakistan. Ravi–It orginates in the Himalayas in the Multhan Tehsil of kangra District of Himachal Pradesh. • It drains the area between the Pir Panjal and the Dhaula Dhar ranges.

question: In India, which of the following rivers of the Indus River system is well known as the habitat of Dolphins?
explanation: • Dolphins are an indicator of a healthy river system. India is one of the few countries in the world that has two fresh water dolphin species– The Gangetic 343 / 896 river dolphin and the Indus river dolphin, sub- species of south Asian river dolphin. IUCN states– ‘Endangered’. • Indus river dolphins are found in Pakistan and River Beas, a tributary of Indus River in Punjab, India. River Beas is the only habitat of Indus River Dolphin in India.

question: According to World Wide Fund, which of the following river of India is one of the most endangered rivers in the world?
explanation: According to World Wide Fund (WWF) is River Ganges, is one of the most endangered rivers in the world. Top–10 endangered rivers of the world are following– (i) Salween, Southeast Asia. (ii) Danube, Europe. (iii) La Plata, South America. (iv) Rio–Grande, North America. (v) Ganga , India. (vi) Indus, India. (vii) Nile–lake Victoria, Africa. (viii) Murray–Darling, Australia. (ix) Mekong–Lancang, China. (x) Yangtze, China.

question: Consider the following pairs : River Tributary 1. Indus Shyok 2. Ganga Gandak 3. Krishna Musi Which of the above pairs is/are correctly matched?
explanation: Tributary of Indus river :– The Indus is the western most river system in the subcontinent. Jhelum, Chenab, Ravi, Beas and Satluj and Shyok are its main tributaries. Tributary of Ganga :– Ramganga, Gomti, Ghagra, Gandak, Koshi and Mahananda etc. Tributary of Krishna River :- Ghataprabha, the Malaprabha, the Bhima, the Musi and the Tungabhadra.

question: Three important names of the Indian sub continent have their sources near the mansarovar Lake in the Great Himalayas : These rivers are
explanation: Three important rivers of the Indian subcontinent have their source near the Mansarovar lake in the Great Himalayas. These rivers are Brahmaputra, Indus and Sutlej. Indian & World Geography

question: Indus river originates in :
explanation: The Indus river arise from the Northen slopes of the Kailash range in Tibet near lake Mansarovar. It enters the Indian Territory in Jammu and Kashmir by forming a Picturesque gorge. It flows between the Ladakh range and Zaskar range at Leh. Major tributaries of Indus river in India are Jhelum, Ravi, Chenab, Beas, Sutlej, Gilgit, Kabul, Kurram, Gomal.

question: A correct sequence of rivers flowing over the Punjab plains is :

options.a: Ganga, Indus, Chenab, Ravi and Sutlej
options.b: Yamuna, Gomti, Ravi, Beas and Sutlej
options.c: Jhelum, Chenab, Ravi, Beas and Sutlej
options.d: Sarda, Kosi, Ravi, Beas and Sutlej

explanation: The Indus receives its most – notable tributaries from the eastern Punjab plain. These five rivers – The Jhelum, Chenab, Ravi, Beas, and Sutlej – give the name Punjab (five rivers) to the region divided between Pakistan and India. Note : The larger part of Punjab plain lies in Pakistan.

question: What is the name of the Brahmaputra River in Tibet?
explanation: The Brahmaputra originates from the Chemayungdung glacier near Mansarovar lake near the Mount Kailash, located on the northern side of the Himalayas in Tibet. 'Yarlung Tsangpo' is the name of the Brahmaputra River in Tibet. In Arunachal Pradesh, Brahmaputra is known by the name 'Dihang'. It is called Jamuna in Bangladesh.

question: India's largest Ropeway over a River was inaugurated in August 2020 on which River it is located?
explanation: India's Longest passenger ropeway over the Brahmaputra river was formally inaugurated in Assam's Guwahati city by state Finance Minister Dr. Himanta Biswa Sharma. (cid:1) The 1.82 km bi-cable ropeway connects Guwahati and North Guwahati and has been constructed at a cost of Rs. 56 crore, (cid:1) The ropeway has a Twin track, Single haul, Bi- cable, Jig-back system with two Cabins. 344 / 896

question: Umananda is a peacock-shaped --------------- in north-east India.
explanation: Umananda is the smallest river island situated near Guwahati, Assam. • It is also known as "Bhasmachal". • It is in the middle of the mighty Brahmaputra and exactly between north and south Guwahati. • It is known as peacock Island, a name given by the British for its peacock feather like structure. • This temple is famous for lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati.

question: The biggest tributary of the Brahmaputra is
explanation: Subansiri :– Several tributaries contribute to Brahmaputra river along its path but Subansiri River is the largest tributary of Brahmaputra River. Burhi Dihing :– Dihing or Burhi Dihing is a large tributary about 380 kilometers long of the Brahmaputra river in upper Assam in northeastern India.

question: At which among the following places, River Brahmaputra takes a U-turn at the time of entering into India?
explanation: Namcha Barwa is a mountain peak in Tibetian Himalaya with the height of 7757 meters. The Tsangpo–Brahmputra river takes a U-turn on reaching the Namcha Barwa and enters India in Arunachal Pradesh through a gorge. Brahmputra River– Is known as Yarlung Tsangpo in Tibet. Siang/Dihang River in Arunachal Pradesh is a 9th Transboundary river. It is the largest river of world. Kula Kangri – Mountain range in Bhutan. Bomdi-la – Between Country Tibet and Tawang district of Arunachal Pradesh. Brahmaputra originated from Angsi Glacier of Himalaya and end in the mouth of Bay of Bengal.

question: Consider the following in relation to the Sundarbans: (A) Sundarbans is the largest deltaic region of the world. (B) It encompasses more than 100 islands. (C) On the southern boundary lies the 24- Parganas district of West Bengal. (D) On the eastern boundary lies Bangladesh. Select the correct answer:
explanation: Sundarbans is a mangrove area in the delta formed by the confluence of the Ganges, Brahmaputra and Meghna Rivers in the Bay of Bengal. It spans from the Hooghly River in India's state of West Bengal to the Baleswar River in Bangladesh's division of Khulna. Indian & World Geography

question: Which of the following is INCORRECTLY matched with respect to river and the corresponding town on its bank?
explanation: River City Brahmaputra - Dibrugarh Narmada - Jabalpur Gomti - Lucknow Chambal - Kota Jabalpur is one of the significant cities in Madhya Pradesh and is immensely popular for it marble rock sites, Dhuandhar Falls and beautiful river bank which is adorned by the waters of river Narmada.

question: Which of the following rivers is not a part of Brahmaputra river system ?
explanation: The Brahmaputra river system (384 km) is one of the longest rivers of the world. It is known as the Yarlung Tsangpo river in Tibet. The Siang/Dihang river in Arunachal Pradesh and Luit, Dilao in Assam, is a transboundary river which flow through Tibet, India and Bangladesh. Majar Tributaries of Brahmaputra River System ● The Subansiri ● The Jiadhal ● The Dharsiri ● The Ruthimari ● The Manas ● The Champamati ● The Sankosh ● The Saral bhanga ● The Pagladiya ● The Aie

question: Kolkata is situated on the banks of the river
explanation: Kolkata is situated on the banks of the river Hooghly. Gomti-Lucknow,Sarayu-Ayodhya , Ganga-Prayagraj, Varanasi , Haridwar

question: Nashik is situated on the banks of the river
explanation: Nashik is situated on the banks of the river Godavari. Note : The Godavari is India's second longest river after the Ganga river. It's source is in Triambakeshwar, Maharashtra. Gomti-Lucknow, Narmada- Jabalpur, Tapti- Surat

question: In India, the river Brahmaputra flows through

options.c: Arunachal Pradesh, Assam and West Bengal

explanation: The river Brahmaputra originates from the Kailash range of Himalayas at an elevation of 5300m. After flowing through Tibet it enters India through Arunachal Pradesh and flows through Assam and Bangladesh before it joins Bay of Bengal. It is called Jamuna in Bangladesh and Yarlung Tsangpo in China 9th and Tibet. It is the largest river in the world by discharge, and the 15th longest.

question: In monohybrid cross a typical genotype ratio is
explanation: A monohybrid cross result in a phenotypic ratio of 3:1 (dominant to recessive), and genotypic ratio of 1:2:1 (homozygous dominant to heterozygous to homozygous recessive).

question: Match the List-I with the List-II and select the correct answer from the given codes : List-I List-II A. Flows between Satpura 1. Mahanadi and the Vindhyan B. Forms a delta in 2. Narmada Odisha C. Hemavati, Herangi and 3. Brahmaputra Amravati are its tributaries D. River also known as 4. Cauvery Tsangpo Code : A B C D
explanation: Narmada is one of the rivers in India that flows in a rift valley flowing west between Satpura and Vindhya Ranges. Other rivers flows through rift valley – Damodar river. Brahmputra river in Tibet called Yarlung Tsangpo. The important tributaries joining the Cauvery are Harangi, Hemavati, Kabini, Suvarnavathi and Bhavani. Cauvery river flows in Karnataka and Tamil Nadu.

question: With which one of the following rivers does Chambal river merge?
explanation: The river Yamuna flows through north-east in Madhya Pradesh, running for a time through Rajasthan, then forming the boundary between Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh before turning south east Chambal river merge with the Yamuna in Uttar Pradesh State.

question: What rate of Interest equalisation is provided to Exporters to boost their export by Govt. of India ?

explanation: Government of India increased the interest 2nd Equalization rate from 3% to 5% on November

question: Earlier 3% rate of equalization was provided to exporters to boost their export by Government of India. 149. Which river of India has the greatest volume of water?
explanation: The Brahmaputra river has the greatest volume of water of all the rivers in India because of heavy annual rainfall levels in its catchment basin. The Brahmaputra called Yarlung Tsangpo in Tibet, Siang/Dihang River in Arunachal Pradesh and Luit, Dilao in Assam is a trans boundary river which flows through Tibet, India and Bangladesh. It is the 9th largest river in the world by discharge and the 15th longest. Rivers Length Ganga 2525km Narmada 1312km Godavari 1465km Brahmaputra 2900km

question: The two head streams of Ganga, the Alaknanda and Bhagirathi which join together at
explanation: The two head streams of Ganga, the Alaknanda and Bhagirathi which join together at Dev Prayag. Alaknanda River + Dhauli Ganga River = Vishnu Prayag. Alaknanda River + Nandakini River = Nanda Prayag Alaknanda River + Pindar River = Karna Prayag Alaknanda River + Mandakini River = Rudra Prayag Alaknanda River + Bhagirathi River = Dev Prayag

question: By which name river Ganga is called in Bangladesh?
explanation: The river Ganga is known as 'Padma' in Bangladesh. Padma River, main channel of the greater Ganges (Ganga) River in Bangladesh. For some 90 miles (145km) the Ganges River forms the western boundary between India and Bangladesh before it enters Bangladesh at the northern edge of the Kushtia district as the upper segment of the Padma River. The upper Padma flows southeastward to receive the mighty Jamuna River (the name of the Brahmaputra in Bangladesh) near Rajbari. 346 / 896

question: Which of the following countries have dispute over Katchatheevu Island?
explanation: Katchatheevu is an uninhabited Island administered by Sri Lanka and was a disputed territory claimed by India until 1976. Ownership of the Island was controversial up until 1974 as during British Rule the Island was administered by both countries. This Island is located between Neduntheevu (Sri Lanka) and Rameshwaram (India). It has been traditionally used by both Sri Lankan Tamil and Indian fishermen. • The Indo-Srilanka agreement allow Indian fisherman to fish around Katchatheevu and day their net on Island.

question: Which of the following rivers feeds the Tehri Dam?
explanation: The Tehri Dam is the tallest dam in India. It is a multi-purpose rock and earth-fill embankment dam on the Bhagirathi river near Tehri in Uttarakhand, India.

question: Which neighbouring country of India celebrates its Independence Day on March 26?
explanation: Bangladesh, the neighbouring country of India, celebrates its independence day on March 26. It commemorates the country's declaration of 26th independence from Pakistan in the late hours of March 1971. While Pakistan and Sri Lanka celebrates its independence day on 14 August and 4 February respectively.

question: Which proportion the Pakistan and India divide the total water of the Indus system?
explanation: The Indus Waters Treaty is a water distribution treaty between India and Pakistan, brokered by the World Bank. It was signed in Karachi on 19 September 1960 by Indian Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru and Pakistani President Ayub Khan. • The treaty gives control over the waters of the three eastern rivers, The Beas, Ravi and Sutlej, to India, while control over the waters of the three Western rivers, the Indus, Chenab and Jhelum, to Pakistan. • India has about 20% of the total water carried by the Indus system while Pakistan has 80%.

question: Which is the longest river of India?
explanation: River Ganga with a length of 2525 km is the longest river of India. It originates from the Gangotri Glacier. The river Yamuna, a major tributary of river Ganges, originates from the Yamunotri glacier near Bandarpoonch. • The Godavari is the longest Peninsular river system. It is also called the Dakshin Ganga. • Brahmaputra originates under the name of siang or Dihang, from the chemayungdung glacier of the Kailash range near the Mansarovar lake. It enters India west of Sadiya town in Arunachal Pradesh.

question: Consider the following statements about the rivers and select the correct answer from the codes given below : 1. Tons is a tributary of Yamuna. 2. Bhilangana is a tributary of Bhagirathi. 3. Pindar is a tributary of Ganga. 4. Mandakini is a tributary of Alaknanda. Codes :
explanation: River Tributary Yamuna - Tons, Chambal, Betwa, Ken (Important Tributaries). Bhagirathi - Bhilangna Ganga - Kali, Yamuna, Kosi, Gandak (Important Tributaries). Alaknanda - Mandakini, Dhauliganga, Pindar

question: India's national river
explanation: The Ganga is India's longest river (2525 km) and is considered to be holiest of river by Hindus. It was declared a National River by Prime Minister Manmohan Singh in 4 November 2008 in order to achieve the objectives of the Ganga Action Plan, initiated by PM Rajiv Gandhi in 1985. It rises from the western Himalaya and drains into the Bay of Bangal. The origin of the Ganga is called as Gangotri

question: The Indo-Gangetic plains, also known as the Great Plains, are large alluvial plains dominated by which of the three main rivers ?
explanation: The Indo-Gangetic plains or the Great Plains are large alluvial plains dominated by three main rivers, the Indus, Ganges, and Brahmaputra.

question: Which is the largest tributary of River Ganga
explanation: The Yamuna is the longest and the second largest tributary river of the Ganga. ⇒ Yamuna Originating from the Yamunotri Glacier at a height of 6387 metres on the south western slope of Bandarpunch peak. Bandarpunch peaks in the upper most region of the lower Himalayas in Uttarakhand, it travels a total length of 1376 kilometers and has a drainage system of 366,223 square kilometers 40.2% of the entire Ganges basin. It is the longest river in India which does not directly flow to the sea.

question: Main river of Ganga drainage system are:
explanation: Bhagirathi river originates from the Gangotri glacier near Gaumukh in Uttarakhand. At Devprayag, the Bhagirathi meets the Alaknanda and then it is known as Ganga . The important tributaries of Ganga are Yamuna, Son, Rihand and Kanhar (tributary of Son), Ghaghra, Kosi, Gandak, Ramganga, Damodar etc.

question: The most polluted river of India is
explanation: Today, The Ganges is considered to be the fifth most polluted river in the world. Raghubir Singh, an Indian photographer has noted that no one in India spoke to the Ganges as being polluted until the late 1970s.

question: Which is longest river of south India?
explanation: The Godavari River is the largest river in peninsular India and known as the 'Dakshina Ganga'. The Godavari Basin is the second largest Basin after the Ganges Basin. Total Length of the river is 1465 km.

explanation: It is one of the largest freshwater lakes in India located in Andhra Pradesh. Kolleru lake lies between the Godavari and Krishna river deltas near the city of Elura. • Pulicat lake is the second largest brackish water lagoon in India after Chilika lake. It is located at the border of Andhra Pradesh and Tamil Nadu. • Wular lake is a fresh water lake. It is situated in Jammu & Kashmir. The lake basin was formed as a result of tectonic activity and is fed by Jhelam river. • Loktak lake is a fresh water lake and it is famous for the phumdis floating over it. It is located in Manipur states.

question: The Dharoi Dam is built over which of the following rivers?
explanation: Dharoi Dam is built over the Sabarmati river in Mehsana district of Gujarat and it is a gravity dam with total height of 45.87 m and total water storage capacity is 908.6 million cubic meter. • This dam constructed in 1978, is used for power generation irrigation drinking water and flood control.

explanation: Indravati is an important tributary of river Godavari and contributes to about 20% of the waters of Godavari. It is the largest and the most important river in Bastar district of Chhattisgarh. The principal tributaries of the river Godavari are- • Pravara • Pranhita • Purna • Sabari • Manjra • Kolab • • Penganga Wainganga • • Wardha Kinnerasani • • Indravati Machkund 348 / 896

question: 'Maneru' river is a tributary of
explanation: The Maner or Manair or Maneru river is a tributary river to the Godavari river in India. The Lower Maner Dam built across this river provides drinking water to Karimnagar, Telangana and also to the NTPC power plant at Ramagundam. Note: Godavari is India's second largest river after Ganga and largest river of South India. Its source is in Trimbakeshwar, Nashik, Maharashtra.

question: Which of the following rivers in India has the largest catchment area?
explanation: The Godavari river is the longest river in peninsular India with a total length of 1465 km. Its water flow area is 312812 sq km. It is the largest peninsular river system. Due to this it is also called the Dakshin Ganga. It rises in the Nasik district of Maharashtra and discharge its water into the bay of Bengal. Its tributaries run through states of Maharashtra, M.P., Chhattisgarh, Odisha, Andhra Pradesh. The drainage area of Mahanadi is 1.42 lakh sq km. Runoff area of Krishna River is 25900 sq km. Narmada River is 18795 sq km. Godavari River in South India is called Dakshin Ganga.

question: Which of these West flowing rivers flow between two mountain ranges?
explanation: Narmada. The Narmada originates in Eastern Madhya Pradesh (India) and flows West across the state, through a narrow valley between the Vindhya Range and spurs of the Satpura range. It flows into the Gulf of Khambhat.

question: Where does river Krishna and river Godavari enter in Telangana respectively?

options.a: Thangidigi Village, Narayanpet District and Kandakurti Village, Nizamabad District
options.b: Gopal Dinne Village, Jogulamba-Gadwal District and Badankurti Village, Nirmal District
options.c: Induvasi Village, Jogulamba-Gadwal District and Chirrakunta Village, Komarambheem- Asifabad District
options.d: Renwatla Village, Narayanpet District and Kalikota Village, Jayashankar- Bhoopalapalli District Indian & World Geography

explanation: 2nd – Godavari is India's largest river after Ganga. Its source is in Triambakeshwar Maharashtra. – The Krishna river is the 4th biggest river in the form of water falls and river basin area in India, after the Ganga, Godavari and Brahamputra. Krishna and Godavari enter in Telangana respectively from Thangidigi Village, Narayanpet District and Kandakurti Village, Nizamabad District.

question: River Krishna acts as a boundary between the states of
explanation: The River Krishna is the second largest east flowing river of peninsula. It originates from the hill of Mahabaleshwar Maharashtra and meets the bay of Bengal in Andhra Pradesh on the east coast. Krishna river forms border between the state of Telangana & Andhra Pradesh from srisailam to pulichintala. * Tributaries of Krishna – Bheema, Tungbhadra, Malprabha Ghatprabha, Musi, Doodhganga, Palaru, Koyna etc.

question: Among the following which one is a North flowing river?
explanation: The Chambal river is North flowing river. Chambal is major tributary of Yamuna river. Chambal rises from Vindhya range from its source it flows north into southeastern Rajasthan state. After entering Uttar Pradesh and runs for 32 km prior to meeting the Yamuna river in Etawah district of U.P.

question: On which of the following rivers is the Dantiwada Dam built?
explanation: The Dantiwada Dam has been constructed across the Banas river. The motive behind the construction of this Dam was to control flood and provide water for the purpose of irrigation projects in Gujarat.

question: Which of the following dams has been constructed across the river Narmada?
explanation: Sardar Sarovor Dam on the Narmada river is located in the village of Kevadin in the state of Gujarat. It is one of the largest and most controversial interstate, multipurpose river valley infrastructure development projects in the country. 349 / 896

question: Which of the following major rivers flow into the Arabian Sea?
explanation: Narmada and Tapti are the two major rivers that fall into the Arabian sea. The Narmada river, also called as the Reva. (cid:1) This river flows in Gujarat and Madhya Pradesh state of India. It is also known as 'Lifeline of Madhya Pradesh and Gujarat' for its huge contribution to the states of Madhya Pradesh and Gujarat in many ways, namely agricultural irrigation, hydro power generation, drinking purposes etc.

question: Which one of the following rivers flows through a rift valley?
explanation: As a rift valley river, the Narmada does not form a delta. Rift valley rivers form estuaries. The other rivers which flow through rift valley include Damodar river, Nagpur plateau and Tapti. The Tapti river and Mahi River also flow through rift valleys, but between different ranges.

question: Which of the following river of India is West flowing?
explanation: Narmada – It is located mainly in Madhya Pradesh. The Narmada river also known as the Rewa River. It is a West flowing river, the Tapi River also flows westwards and drains into the Arabian Sea. Tributaries are – Kolar River, Shakkar River, Dudhi, Tawa and Hiran River. Damodar River– Jharkhand and West Bengal. The Cauvery is an Indian river flowing through the states of Karnataka and Tamil Nadu. Tala Kaveri is the source of river Cauvery. Tungabhadra River is a river in India that starts and flows through the state of Karnataka, source of the river is Tungabhadra.

question: From where does Penna river originate?
explanation: The Penna rises in the Nandi Hills in Chikkaballapur district of Karnataka state, and runs North and East in Karnataka, South towards Tamilnadu with the Tamil name "Then Pennai" and east through the states of Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh, known as Uttara Pinakini, to empty into the Bay of Bengal. • Godavari River is originated from Brahmagiri hill in the western Ghat range in Maharashtra. Indian & World Geography

question: Average water resource available of the Mahanadi basin is?
explanation: The average water resource potential of the basin is 66.9 BCM. The utilizable potential of Mahanadi basin is 50 BCM (CWC, 2014). In terms of water storage, the Basin has total storage capacity of 14207.80 MCM.

question: Which of the following statements are INCORRECT regarding river Luni? 1. Luni is largest river in the Thar desert in north-west India 2. It originates in the Pushkar valley of the Aravalli Range, near Ajmer 3. The River ends in the marshy lands of the Ranna of Kutch in Gujarat 4. This river flows in Patan, Banaskantha, Sabarkanta, and Surendranagar
explanation: The Luni is the largest river in the Thar Desert of North-West India. It originates in the Pushkar Valley of the Aravalli Range, near Ajmer passes through the southeastern portion of the Thar Desert and ends in the Marshy lands of Rann of Kutch in Gujarat after travelling a distance of 495 km.

question: Among the following rivers which river has an estuary?
explanation: The Tapi is the second longest West flowing river of the peninsular India and is known as the twins of Narmada which makes estuaries. Tapi is a major river in Central India. The length of this river is 724 km.

question: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding river Narmada? 1. It is the fifth largest river in the country 2. It traverses Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra and Gujarat 3. The total length of the river from source to sea is 2400 kilometers
explanation: The Narmada River also called the Reva and previously also known as Narmada or anglicised as 5th Nerbudda is the longest River in India and largest flowing River of Madhya Pradesh. The total length of the River from source to sea is 1312 km.

question: Which place is situated at a confluence of three rivers–Kapila, Saraswati and Hiranya?
explanation: Bhalka Tirtha is sacred teerth is located 5 km on Prabhas Veraval Highway. Bhalka Tirtha located in the veraval in Saurashtra on the Western coast of Gujarat India is the place where Krishna was killed by an arrow shot by a hunter named jara, after which he worshiped Shiva an act referred to in the Puranas as Shri Krishna Nijdham Prasthan Leela.

question: Which of the following is correct about the river Narmada? (i) The Narmada has formed the Delta (ii) The Narmada has formed the Estuary (iii) It originates from the State of Andhra Pradesh
explanation: • The Narmada River originates from Narmada Kund located in Amarkantak, Annupur district in M.P. • The Narmada river forms the unmarked boundary between North and South India. • Narmada is the 5th largest river in the country and the largest one is Gujarat that does not form the Delta, it form Estuary.

question: Which river of the following traverses through Andhra Pradesh?
explanation: Andhra Pradesh is a revering state with 40 major, medium and minor rivers. Godavari, Krishna, Vamsadhara, Nagavali and Pennar are major interstate rivers in the given option Pennar river traverses through Andhra Pradesh after traversing 13 km. in Tumkur district again it reenters in Anatapur district.

question: On which of the following river systems is Sardar Sarover Project built
explanation: The Sardar Sarovar Project is the largest in the Narmada valley. Sardar Sarovar dam is the largest of the 30 dams to be build on the Narmada river. It is built at a place called Navagam in Bharuch district of Gujarat state. Note:- This dam supplies water and electricity in four states Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Rajasthan.

question: Which among the following rivers does not flow from East to West?
explanation: Narmada, Tapti and Mahi river flows from East to West. Narmada, Tapti flows through a rift valley while Kaveri River originates from the Brahmagiri hill of Kodagu district of Karnataka and flows towards Bay of Bengal. It is also called 'Dakshin ki Ganga'. Kaveri river carries water throughout the year. Cauvery river Indian & World Geography does not flow from East to West. The river flows from West to East. • The river that flow from south to north is Narmada Chambal on and Tungabhadra.

question: Which one of the following rivers drains into Rann of Kutch?
explanation: Luni rivers drains into Rann of Kutch. The Rann of Kutch is a salt marsh in the Thar desert of Gujarat. It rises Pushkar valley of the Aravali Range in central Rajasthan and flows some 530km West-Southwest into the Rann of Kutch in Gujarat State. Luni is also called the salt river.

question: Which of the following is a tributary of the Ravi river?
explanation: Ravi river rises from the Bara Banghal (a branch of Dhauladhar) as a joint stream formed by the glacier-fed Badal and Tant Gari. The right bank tributaries of the Ravi river are the Budhil, Tundahan, Beljedi Saho and Siul and its left bank tributary is Chirchind Nala.

question: Wullar lake is situated on river
explanation: Wullar lake is one of the largest fresh water lake in Asia. It is situated in Bandipora district of Jammu & Kashmir. It's basin was formed as a result of Tectonic activity & is fed by river Jhelum. It is one of the 46 Ramsar sites in India. Wullar Lake also has a small island in its center called the 'Zaina Lank'.

question: The river Tapti originates at :
explanation: Tapti river originates from Multai in Betul district of Madhya Pradesh in the Satpura range. • The ancient name of the Tapti rivers is the Tapi river. It is one of the major river of peninsular India.

question: How much total area of Narmada basin?
explanation: Sorrow of Bihar is Koshi River. The river gained its name as the sorrow of Bihar due to the annual floods that affect the state most of the times during the rainy season. Damodar River is known as "River of Sorrows and Sorrow of Bengal."

question: Pranahita is a
explanation: Pranahita river is a largest tributary of Godavari River. It flows along the border of Gadchiroli district in Maharashtra and Adilabad district in Telangana.

question: 'Pranahita' river consists of which of the following rivers?

options.a: Pen Ganga, Wardha and Waine Ganga

explanation: The Pranahita river is the largest tributary of Godavari river covering about 34% of its drainage basin conveying the combined waters of the Penganga River, the Wardhas River and the Wainganga River. Note:- The Pranhita river of follows a short course of 113 Kilometers, strictly adhering to the boundary between Gadchiroli District in Maharashtra and Adilabad district in Telangana.

question: Which among the following is NOT a west flowing river in India?
explanation: The Mahanadi is a major river in east central India. It drains an area of around 141, 600 sq. km. and has a total course of 858 km. Mahanadi is also known for Hirakund Dam. The river flows through the states of Chhattisgarh and Odisha. West Flowing River– Indus, Narmada, Tapti, Sabarmati, Mahi, Ghaggar, Luni, Saravati, Pumba, Periyar, Bharathapuzha. East Flowing River– Ganga, Brahmaputra, Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Cauvery, Penner, Ponnaiyar, Vaigai and Subarnarekha.

question: Match List-I with List-II correctly and select you answer using the codes given below. Peninsular Rivers Tributaries
explanation: Matching the list Ist with IInd. Peninsular Rivers Tributaries Mahanadi → Sheonath or Shivnath river Godavari → Penganga Krishna → Bhima Kavery → Hemavati • Tributaries of Mahanadi : The Sheonath, the Jonk, the Hasdeo, the Mand, the ib, the Ong and the Tel. • Tributaries of Godavari : The Pravara, the Purna, the Manjra, the Penganga, the Wardha, the Wainganga, the Pranhita. • Tributaries of Krishna : The Ghataprabha, the Malaprabha, the Bhima and the Tungabhadra. • Tributaries of Cauvery : The Harangi, the Hemavati, the Kabini, the Suvarnavathi and the Bhavani.

question: Khurja hill is the place of origin of which river?
explanation: The Ib river originates in Khurja hills near Pandrapata at an elevation of 762 metres. It passes through Raigarh district and Jashpur district of Chhattisgarh and Sundargarh district of Odisha and finally meets Mahanadi at Hirakud Dam. The river runs for a distance of about 252 kilometres. It is major tributary of Mahanadi river.

question: Tel river flows through which tehsil?
explanation: The Tel river flow in Deobhog Tehsil of Chhattisgarh. Tel is an important tributary of Mahanadi.

question: Which of the following river is not a tributary of river Indravati
explanation: Kanger is not a tributary of Indravati River. Kanger river is perennial river of Bastar. Kanger National Park is located on its side. Hills are recharging reservoir of Kanger River, Kanger river irrigates the whole valley in its natural resources.

question: Which one of the following rivers does not flow through Uttarakhand?
explanation: Alaknanda river, Yamuna river, Bhagirathi, Mandakini, Ganga (Bhagirathi and Alaknanda), Dhauliganga, Sarda river, Pindar, Tons, Sarayu, Nandakini, Bhilangna, Rishganga, Gori ganga, Jadh Ganga etc.flow from Uttarakhand. Kaveri River flow through the states of Karnataka and Tamilnadu and descends the eastern ghats in a series of great falls. 352 / 896

question: Amaravati river is a tributary of
explanation: The Amaravati River is the longest tributary of Cauveri River in the fertile districts of Karur and Tirupur of Tamil Nadu state. • Tributaries of Kaveri Rivers are Hemavati, Shimsha, Kabini, Bhavani etc. • Cauveri River is known as the Ganges of South India.

question: The Cauvery river or Kaveri, is one of the important and sacred rivers of South India and travels through Karnataka and Tamil Nadu across the Deccan Plateau. Which of the following statements is/are true about it?

options.a: It originates at Talakaveri in the Eastern Ghats Mountain Range.
options.b: Jog Falls is located on the Shimoga and Uttara Kannada district border is formed by it.
options.c: Hemavati river is one of its tributaries.
options.d: Kabini river is not one of its tributaries.

explanation: The Cauvery or Kaveri is an Indian river flowing through the states of Karnataka and Tamil Nadu. The Kaveri River rises at Talakaveri in the Brahmagiri range in the Western Ghats, Kodagu district of Karnataka. Tributaries of Kaveri River is Hemavati, Harangi, Kabini, Bhavani, Hakshmana, Tirtha, Noyyal and Arkavati. Note– Jog Falls is a waterfall on the Sharavati River located in the Western Ghats near Sagar Taluk of Shivamogga district, Karnataka. It is the second highest plunge waterfall in India.

question: In which district does the Surahi River flow?

options.e: None of the other options Indian & World Geography

explanation: Surahi River flows in Rajnandgaon, district of Chhattisgarh state. This river is situated at the extreme end of the district. Surahi River is the life-giving river of Gandai.

question: Another name of Mahanadi is:
explanation: Another name of Mahanadi is- Chitrotpala Ancient era – Kanaknandini Dvapara yuga – Chitrotpala (Also in Matasya Purana) Treta yuga – Nilotpala (Also in Vayu Purana)

question: The River Cauvery originates from which of the following states ?
explanation: The River Cauvery (Kaveri) originates from Karnataka. Talakaveri, the place where river Kaveri originated, is situated on a hill called Brahmagiri, covered with dense forests in the Western Ghats of Karnataka, which is also the heart of Coorg District.

question: The 'Bist Doab' spreads which of the following rivers?
explanation: The plains situated between two rivers which meet at some place are called Doab. The Doab Bist lies between the Sutlej and the Beas River. The river Sutlej separates Doab from the Malwa region and the Beas river separates Doab from the Majha region.

question: Which of the following rivers is NOT a tributary of the Yamuna river?
explanation: The Yamuna river originated from the Yamunotri glacier near Bandarpunch in Uttarkashi district of Uttarakhand. Yamuna is the largest tributary of the Ganga. The main Tributaries of Yamuna are Tons, Chambal, Kalsindh, Betwa, Ken, Hindon, Rihand etc. Gandak is a Tributary of the Ganges.

question: The entire Surajpur district falls under which of the following drainage basins?
explanation: Surajpur district falls under the Ganga drainage basins. • Surajpur situated beside the Rehar/Rihand/Renkua river. • Rihand river is a tributary of the Son River which is also the tributary of River Ganga. 353 / 896

question: Shivri Narayan is located on the banks of which river?
explanation: Shivri Narayan is located on the bank of Mahanadi, Jonk and Shivnath rivers. • Shivrinarayan was named after lady Shabri. • Shivrinarayan has a significant role in the life of Lord Rama.

question: "Doab" means
explanation: Land between two tributaries of a river means "Doab". The place where two small streams joint together and results in the formation of a big river is called a DOAB.

question: How many riparian states are there in Godavari Basin?
explanation: The Godavari River has its catchment area in seven states of India viz. Andhra Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Telangana, Chhattisgarh, Karnataka, Odisha, Maharashtra. Note:- Godavari basin is the highest among all the river basins in India. The Godavari is India's second longest river after the Ganga, Its source is in Triambakeshwar, Maharashtra. It flows east for 1465 Kms.

question: In the place called 'Somnath' which rivers confluence
explanation: Shivnath or Somnath is the tributary of Mahanadi River. Kharun River flows and joins Shivnath River at Somnath. The temple is situated at Simona of Raipur Chhattisgarh.

question: Which one of the following cities does not lie on the bank of Ganga?
explanation: Agra is a city on the banks of the river Yamuna in the northern state of Uttar Pradesh, India. It is 378 km west of the state capital, Lucknow, 206 km south of the national capital New Delhi. It is 24th populous city in India.

question: Which of the following cities and rivers are not properly matched?
explanation: Bhopal is the administrative capital of Madhya Pradesh is not located on the bank of any river. (cid:1) The city of Kota lies on the east bank of the river Chambal in Southern Rajasthan. (cid:1) Kanpur is a major industrial town of Uttar Pradesh, the northern state of India. This town is situated on the south bank of River Ganga. (cid:1) Agra city western Uttar Pradesh state, northern India. It lies in the Indo-Gangetic Plain on the Yamuna river about 125 miles (200Km) South-East of Delhi.

question: 'Sambalpur' is situated on the bank of which of following rivers?
explanation: Sambalpur lies on the bank of the river Mahanadi. The river flows to the west of the city and separates Burla from Sambalpur and Hirakund. The Hirakund Dam lies upstream of Sambalpur. • Delhi, Agra are located on the bank of river Yamuna. • Ayodhya is located on the bank of river Saryu.

question: The source of River Vaigai is in the hills of
explanation: The source of River Vaigai is in the hills of Varushanad (Tamil Nadu). The river flows through Tamil Nadu. It passes through, the towns of Madurai, Andipatti and Theni.

question: Utkal plains include
explanation: The Utkal Plain is part of the East Coastal Plain of India. It is a coastal plain in the Odisha state of eastern India. It includes the delta of the Mahanadi river, Brahmani river, Baitarani river. The most prominent physiographic feature of this plain is the Chilka lake.

question: The highest peak in the Eastern Ghats is
explanation: Mahendragiri is the highest peak in the eastern ghats which is situated in Paralakhemundi, Gajapati district of Odisha. It is situated amongst the Eastern Ghats at an elevation of 1,501 metres. Anamudi is also known as Everest of South India. Kanchenjunga is the 3rd highest mountain peak in the world. Khasi hills are found on the Shilong plateau.

question: What is the coastline length of Andhra Pradesh?
explanation: The state of Andhra Pradesh has the second- longest coastline in India and the coastline is 974 km long along with the Coromandel Coast between the Eastern Ghat and Bay of Bengal. • This coastline has rich agricultural land due to the delta- Godavari, Krishna River and Penna. • The total length of India's coastline is 7516.6 kilometers. (cid:1) Gujarat has the longest coastline in India and its length is 1600 km. (cid:1) Tamil Nadu is the third largest coastline in India and is known as the Coromandel Coast. (cid:1) 4th The largest coastline – 720 km and is known as Konkan Coast. (cid:1) 5th largest coastline- Kerala, length 580 km and is known as Malabar Coast.

question: Government of India planned to setting up new major port 'Vadhavan' in the state of
explanation: The Union cabinet chaired by Prime Minister Narendra Modi has given its in principle approval for setting up a new major port at Vadhavan near Dahanu in Maharashtra. The New major port will be established on the basis of Land Lord Model.

question: Which of the following ports is located on the Eastern Coast of India?
explanation: Kamarajar Port is located on the eastern coast (Coromandel Coast) about 24 km north of Chennai Port 12th (Chennai), it is the major port of India and the first port in India which is a public company. The port was declared as a major port under the Indian Ports Act, 1908 in March 1999 and incorporated as Ennore Port Limited under the Companies Act, 1956 in October

question: The Kamarajar Port is the only corporatized major port and is registered as a company. Nhava Sheva port– also known as Jawaharlal Nehru port, located Navi Mumbai Maharashtra. Mormugao port– It is one of the India’s oldest port, located in Goa. 226. Major port of the East coast of India
explanation: The major ports on the east coast are : Tuticorin (Tamil Nadu), Vishakhapatnam (Andhra Pradesh), Ennore (Tamil Nadu), Chennai (Tamil Nadu), Haldia and Kolkata (West Bengal) and Port Blair (Andaman and Nicobar Islands). Kandla, Karaikal & Puducherry are located on the west coast of India. • Kandla port has been renamed as Deendayal Upadhyay port. Indian & World Geography

question: Utkal Plains is located in which plain regions of India _____
explanation: The Utkal plain is a part of the Eastern coastal plain of India. It is the coastal plain in Odisha state. It includes the delta of the Mahanadi River, Brahmani River, Baitarni River etc. Famous Chilika lake is located in this plain. Chilika is the largest saltwater lake in India.

question: The fishing port of Tuticorin is located on-
explanation: The fishing port of Tuticorin is located on the coromandel coast. This port has a natural harbour and rich hinterland. Thus, it has a flourishing trades handling of a large variety of cargoes to even our neighboring countries like Sri Lanka, Maldives, etc and the Coastal regions of India. Some important Ports in India- • Tuticorin Port (Tamil Nadu) • Jawaharlal Nehru Port (Maharashtra) • Mormugao Port (Goa) • Paradip Port (Orissa) • Panambur Port (Karnataka) • Kandla Port (Gujarat) • Cochin Port (Kerala)

question: Fishing is highly developed along the Western Coast of India as

options.a: The continental slope along the Western Coast is well developed.
options.b: The continental shelf along the Western Coast is well pronounced.
options.c: The continental slope along the Western Coast is not well developed.
options.d: The continental shelf along the Western Coast is not well developed.

explanation: The West Coast of India is by far the most important area as far as fisheries is concerned accounting for the 70 percent of National Production. This is a result of both major upwelling areas in the southwest and productive shallow water demersal resources in the northwest. Due to this marked or pronounced continental shelf along the western coast of India fishing is highly productive.

question: Sahyadri is the traditional name of the
explanation: The Western Ghats, also known as Sahyadri, are a mountain range that covers an area of 140,000 square kilometer in a stretch of 1,600 kilometer parallel to the western coast of Indian peninsula, traverse the states of Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Goa, Maharashtra and Gujarat. Eastern Ghats are a discontinuous range of mountains along India’s eastern coasts and pass through Odisha, Andhra Pradesh to Tamilnadu. Aravalli Range is located in Northern-Western India, passes through Delhi, Haryana, Rajasthan and Gujrat. Nagarjuni hills are located near Gaya, Bihar less than two km from Barabar Hills in Jehanabad district in Bihar. They are famous for Rock-cut caves with Mauryan inscriptions.

question: Which among the following are the IUCN biodiversity hotspots in India? 1. The Himalayas 2. The Western Ghats 3. The Indo-Burma region 4. The Sundaland (near Nicobar group of Islands) Choose the correct answer using the following codes given below :
explanation: India hosts 4 of 34 globally identified biodiversity hotspots. The Himalayas, the Western Ghats, the Indo-Burma region and the Sundaland (Includes Nicobar group of Island). • India became a state member of IUCN in 1969, through the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC). • IUCN Stand for International Union for Conservation of Nature.

question: The Meghalaya Plateaus are largely formed of ................ .
explanation: The Meghalaya plateaus are largely formed of Gondwana rocks. Gondwana rocks : The lower Gondwana rocks of permo-carboniferous age are recongnized at the western part of Garo Hills and consists of pebble bed, sandstone and carbonaceous shale. The Cretaceous lava : Tertiary sediments occupying southern part of the Meghalaya plateau comprises of the Khasi group (arenaceous facies), the Jaintia group (calcareous facies) and the youngest formation the Garo group which is represented as Simsang, Bagmara and Chengapara formations.

question: Which one of the following mountain chains has two dissimilar types of vegetation (evergreen forests and deciduous forests) on its two slopes?
explanation: The Western Ghats is mountain range that km2 covers as an area of 160,000 travelling the states of Karnataka, Goa, Maharashtra, Gujarat and Tamil Nadu. It is a UNESCO World heritage site in 2010 and it is one of the eight biodiversity hotspot in the world. The northern part of the Western Ghat range are much drier as compared to southern parts, as they have mountains type of vegetation and the north have a deciduous type.

question: In which of the following states is Hazira Port located?
explanation: Hazira Port is located in the state of Gujarat in Western India (at western coast). Hazira is most important part of Surat Metropolitan area. Hence it is called the "Industrial Hub" of India. • Major Ports of India:- Kandla Port (Gujarat), Mumbai Port , Jawahar Lal Nehru Port (Mumbai), Mormugao Port (Goa), New Mangaluru Port (Karnataka), Cochin Port (Kerala), Kolkata Port (West Bengal), Visakhapatnam Port (Andhra Pradesh), Paradip Port (Odisha), Chennai Port (Tamil Nadu), Kamarajar Port (Tamil Nadu), V.O. Chidambaranar Port (Tamil Nadu).

question: Which of the following ports is the first port that was developed after independence in Gujrat State?
explanation: Kandla, also known as Kandla Port Trust or Deendayal Port is a seaport in Kutch district of Gujarat State in Western India, near the city of Gandhidham. Kandla was constructed in 1950 as the chief sea port serving western India, after the partition of India & Pakistan left the port of Karachi in Pakistan.

question: Which of the following geographical/natural regions is declared "World Heritage Site"?
explanation: In July 2012, a total of 39 areas in the Western Ghats, including national parks, wildlife sanctuaries and reserved forests were designated as 'World Heritage Site'. The Western Ghats extend from Dang in Maharashtra- Gujarat to near Kanyakumari in Tamil Nadu, it is spread over 7,953 sq km older than the Himalaya. The Western Ghats Mountain chain is recognized as one of the world’s eight “hottest hot spots” of biological diversity.

question: Rainshadow areas of Western Ghats have
explanation: A rainshadow is an area of significantly reduced rainfall behind a mountainous region, on the side facing away from prevailing winds, known as its leeward side. The strip of land between east of the western Ghats and the Cardamom Hills experiences tropical semi-arid (steppe) climate. Due to rainshadow of the Western Ghat this region receives minimum rainfall (40 to 75 cm annually). Region includes Karnataka, Western Tamil Nadu, Western Andhra Pradesh and Central Maharashtra. Tropical wet and dry climate: Most of the Deccan plateau has this climate. The annual rainfall from monsoon is between 75 to 150 cm. Humid subtropical climate: It is experienced in the foot hills of the Himalayas especially Punjab-Haryana plain, in Rajasthan towards the east of the Aravalli range and in Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Northern part of West Bengal and Assam. The annul rainfall between 65 cm to 250 cm. Arid subtropical climate: Western Rajasthan with scanty rainfall in this region.

question: The sea coast extending between Krishna river delta and Kanyakumari is -
explanation: Coromandal coast ranges from Kanyakumari in Tamilnadu to False Divi point in Andhra Pradesh, which is just at the apex of the Krishna River delta. The Region between the Krishna and Mahanadi river for which an old ten Northern circars is sometimes used. Some classify it as Andhra Pradesh coastal plains.

question: What is the special feature of the western coastal plain of India?

options.a: Wide continental shelf, mudflats and coral reefs
options.c: Backwaters, Narrow continental shelf and coral reefs
options.d: Shallow continental shelf, mud flats and coral reefs
options.e: Deltas, deep continental shelf and backwaters

explanation: Western coastal plain of India lies between Western Ghats and Arbian sea, approximately 80–100 km wide. It has coral reefs, mudflats, and wide continental shelf. It is 150m to 300m above from sea level.

question: Which one of the following is NOT a Andaman and Nicobar groups of Islands?
explanation: Amindivi Island is a northern group of the Lakshadweep. Narcondam Island, Bompuka Island, Rutland Island are a group of a Andaman and Nicobar Islands. Indian & World Geography

question: How many islands are there in Lakshadweep ?
explanation: Lakshadweep, the group of 36 Islands is known for its exotic and sun-kissed beaches and lush green landscape. India's smallest Union Territory Lakshadweep is an archipelago with an area of 32 sq km. It is an uni- district Union Territory and is comprised of 12 atolls, 3 reefs, 5 submerged banks and 10 inhabited islands. The Capital of Lakshadweep is Kavaratti. Lakshadweep has a tropical climate and it has an 27oC–32oC, average temperature of and from June to October the South-West Monsoon is active with an average rainfall of 10-40 mm.

question: Which one among the following is the coral group of islands situated in India?
explanation: • Lakshadweep is about 220 to 440 kms away from the coast of Kerala, lying one of the world’s most spectacular tropical Islands systems in a scattered group of 36 coral islands. These Islands have been built up by corals and have fringing coral reefs very close to their shores. • The Andaman Islands are an Indian archipelago in the Bay of Bengal. These roughly 300 islands are known for their palm-lined, white-sand beaches, mangroves and tropical rainforests.

question: The Indian island that lies between India and Sri Lanka is
explanation: The Pamban Island also known as Rameswaram Island, is an island located between peninsular India and Sri Lanka, on the Adam's Bridge archipelago. The chain extends between Dhanushkodi on Pamban Island. The second largest Island in the latter, Pamban Island belongs to India and forms the Rameswaram taluk of the Ramanathapuram district of the state of Tamil Nadu.

question: The most spoken language of Andaman and Nicobar Islands is
explanation: Bengali is the most spoken language in the Andaman and Nicobar Island. Hindi is the official language of the Andaman and Nicobar Island, while english is declared as additional official language for communication purpose. 357 / 896

question: Shivasamudram is an Island town situated on which river?
explanation: Shivasamudram is an Island town situated on Kaveri River. • Shivasamudram Hydel Project is located at Kaveri River. • In 1905, Asia's first hydro-electric project was set up here.

question: Port Blair is located on which of the following Islands?
explanation: Port Blair is the capital of Union Territory of Andaman & Nicobar Islands. It is located on the east coast of the South Andaman Island.

question: 'Floating Islands' are particular feature of one of the lakes in India which is
explanation: Loktak Lake : Loktak Lake is the largest fresh water lake in North East India. It is famous for the phumdis floating over it. Renuka Lake : It is in the Siramour district of Himachal Pradesh in India. It is the largest lake in Himachal Pradesh. Bhimtal Lake : It is the largest lake in Kumaon region known as the "lake district in India" situated in the State of Uttarakhand. Pushkar Lake : It is located in the town of Pushkar in Ajmer district of the Rajasthan state of western India. It is sacred lake of the Hindus.

question: In which Island of Indian Ocean the U.S. Naval base is located?
explanation: Diego Garcia is an Island of the British Indian Ocean Territory. U.S. Naval base is located at Diego Garcia and United States built a large naval and military base. Okinawa : It is a Japanese Island in the East China Sea. La Reunion : It is an Indian ocean Island in East Africa, east of Madagascar, southwest of Mauritius. Seychelles : It is an archipelagic Island country in the Indian Ocean.

question: Which one of the following is referred to as 'Gold mine of timber'?
explanation: Andaman Islands is known as 'Gold mine of timber' because of its rain forests. Andaman forest contains 200 or more timber producing species. • The only Commercial Airport is Veer Savarkar International Airport in Port Blair.

question: The biggest island in Indian Ocean is :
explanation: Madagascar is the biggest island in the Indian Ocean with an area of about 226, 658 square miles. It is the fourth largest in the world after Greenland, New Guinea and Borneo. • Madagascar lies 250 miles off the Eastern Cost of Southern Africa.

question: Which one of the following states has the longest coastline?
explanation: India has a coastline of 7516.6 km. 5422.6 km of mainland coastline and 2094 km of island territories, 1214.7 kilometers long coastline of Gujarat, which is approximately 23% of the country’s total mainland coastline, make it the state with the longest mainland coastline. Andhra Pradesh has total coastline length 972 km., Maharashtra total coastline is 720 km and Odisha total coastline is 485 km.

question: Number of coastal States and coastal Union Territories (Excluding Island Union Territories) in India is:
explanation: Indian coastline touches nine states – Gujarat, Maharashtra, Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Odisha, West Bengal and two Union territories- Daman and Diu and Puducherry.

question: Which of the following States has shortest coastline?
explanation: Goa is the smallest Indian State with a coastline of 160 km long. • Maharashtra State has 720 km long coastline. • Gujarat has the longest coastline in India which lies in the Kathiawar region of the state (1600 km long). • Total 9 coastal states are in India– Gujarat, Maharastra, Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Odisha, West Bengal and two union territories Daman & Diu and Puducherry. • The two territories of India, Andaman and Nicobar Islands and Lakshadweep have coastline other than mainland. 358 / 896

question: Palghat joins which of the following states?
explanation: Palakkad Gap or Palaghat Gap is a low mountain pass in the Western Ghats between Coimbatore in Tamil Nadu and Palakkad in Kerala. The pass is located between the Nilgiri Hills to the north and Anaimallai Hills to the south. This is the lowest pass through the Western Ghats and acts as an important transport corridor between Tamil Nadu and Kerala. Thal Ghat – Mumbai-Nashik Bhor Ghat – Mumbai-Pune

question: What is the approximate length of coast line of Andaman & Nicobar Islands in Kilometers?
explanation: The Islands of Andaman and Nicobar has the longest coastline in India, amounting to 1912 km followed by Gujarat (1600 km). Union Territory of Daman & Diu (27 km) has smallest coastline.

question: The Bombay High oil fields are located on which one of the following submarine relief features?
explanation: The Bombay High oil fields are located on continental shelf relief submarine relief feature. It is an offshore oilfield 176 km, of the west coast of Mumbai in Gulf of Cambay region of India, in about 75m of water. • Continental slope is slope between the outer edge of the continental shelf and the deep ocean floor. • Continental shelf is a portion of a continent that is submerged under an area of relatively shallow water known as a shelf sea. • Continental rise is a wide, gentle incline from a deep ocean plain (abyssal plain) to a continental slope. • Ocean Basin floor is every where covered by sediments of different types of origins.

question: The valley of Kashmir lies between ---------- .

options.a: Pir Panjal range and Dhola Dhar range
options.c: Karakoram range and Pir Panjal range
options.d: Pir Panjal range and the Himadri range

explanation: Kashmir valley also known as the vale of Kashmir is a valley in the portion of the Kashmir region administered by India. It is approximately 135 km long and 32 km wide and is drained by the Jhelum River.

question: Where is Kass Valley, also known as 'valley of flowers', located?
explanation: The Kass Valley also known as Valley of Flowers is located in Satara city in Maharashtra. The place has some of the rarest flowers available in the country. The primary attraction of the Kaas Valley is the sea of flowers which blooms soon after the rainy season.

question: The "silent valley" is located in which of the following states?
explanation: Silent Valley is National Park located in Kerala. It has an area of 89.54 sq.km and located in the Nilgiri hills.

question: Which of the following is not a major objective of a multipurpose river valley project?

question: Match the following : List - I A. Oligotrophic lakes B. Dystrophic lakes C. Meromictic lakes D. explanation: Kolleru Lake is located in state of Andhra Pradesh. It was designated as Ramsar Site in 2002 under the International Ramsar Convention.

question: The biggest lake in India is
explanation: (i) Chilika Lake–It is the largest coastal lagoon in India and the second largest lagoon in the world. Chilika Lake is the largest wintering ground for migratory birds on the Indian sub continent. It is a brackish water coastal lake. (ii) Dal Lake–It is a lake in Srinagar and is integral to tourism known as the jewel in the crown of Kashmir or Srinagar's jewel. Asia's largest tulip garden is found on the bank of Dal Lake. Mughal garden, Shalimar bagh and the Nishat Bagh are located on the banks of Dal Lake. Indian & World Geography (iv) Vembanad Lake–Longest lake in India and the largest lake in the state of Kerala. The Nehru trophy boat race is conducted in a portion of the lake.

question: In India, rainforests are mainly found in
explanation: Tropical Rainforests of India, are found in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, the Western Ghats, the coastline of peninsular India, and the greater Assam region in the north-east. Small remnants of rainforest are found in Odisha state. • Areas that receive more than 200 cm of rainfall and have a short dry season are the best area for tropical rainforests. •The North East Region receives heavy to very heavy rainfall during the south-west monsoon season (June to September) and rain peak in June. • The North- East Rainforests stretch from Northern Assam to Nagaland, Manipur, Mizoram, Tripura, and Part of Arunachal Pradesh. • Elephants, Monkeys, Lemur, and Deer are the common animals in these forests.

question: The south-west monsoon season extends from?

question: The rain-fed and rain-shadow areas, are the characteristics of which type of the following rainfall?
explanation: The rain-fed and rain shadow areas are the characteristics of orographic rainfall. • The Western Ghats and North East region of India are two location that receive the highest annual rainfall in India. • Both of these region have the unique characteristics of mountainous terrain. This hilly terrain acts as barrier to South West winds. This leads to intensified downpour at the windward side of the mountains. This type of rainfall is known as orographic rainfall.

question: In India, the tropical deciduous forests form the natural cover in nearly all the places where the annual rainfall is between ............. .
explanation: In India, the tropical deciduous forests form the natural cover in nearly all the places where there is annual rainfall between 101 cm and 200 cm.

question: In India Retreating Monsoon winds start from.
explanation: Retreating monsoon season commences with the beginning of the withdrawal of the south-west monsoon (mid-September-November) and lasts till early January. It is a 3-month long process where it starts from the peninsula in October and from the extreme south-eastern tip by December.

question: Which of the following statements is/are correct? 1. The withdrawal of monsoon is much more rapid phenomena than its arrival 2. The withdrawal of monsoon is much more gradual phenomena than its arrival 3. Within monsoon region there is high reliability and high variability of rainfall
explanation: The withdrawal of monsoon is much more gradual phenomena than its arrival, within monsoon region there is high reliability and high variability of rainfall.

question: Mawsynram is famous for:
explanation: Mawsynram is world famous for the highest rainfall (over 1080 cm), located on the Summit (peak) of Khasi hills in Meghalaya.

question: From which of the following winds does major part of India get rainfall during the monsoon season?
explanation: India's major part receives most of its rainfall from the south west monsoon winds, and it is referred to as the period June to September. These winds are flow from the Arabian Sea towards India's mainland in the south west direction. Whole country receives nearly 75-80% of its rainfall during this period. • Tamil Nadu receives little rainfall from the south- west monsoon cause of rain shadow region.

question: The average rainfall in Assam during the monsoon season is
explanation: Regions of North-East receives maximum rainfall from monsoon rain. The average rainfall in Assam during the monsoon season is between 170-180 cm. Average rainfall in India is about 120-125 cm. Mawsynram is received 11,871mm rainfall annually making it the place with highest rainfall in the world. While Kutch region of Gujarat and Rajasthan are the lowest rainfall state in India.

question: Which Monsoon area does India comes under?
explanation: True monsoon areas include India, Myanmar (Burma), Pakistan, Bangladesh, Thailand, Cambodia, Laos, North and South Vietnam, Southern China, Philippines and Northern Coastal area of Australia. question: Yokohama strategy and plan of action was associated with
explanation: The First World Conference on Natural Disasters in Yokohama, Japan from May 23 to 27, 1994, adopted the Yokohama Strategy for a Safer World : Guidelines for Natural Disaster Prevention, Preparedness and Mitigation and its Plan of Action, endorsed by the UN General Assembly in 1994.

question: From which branch of monsoon does Chhattisgarh get its maximum rainfall?
explanation: Chhattisgarh receives its maximum rainfall by Bay of Bengal branch. • Amount of rainfall decreases from East to West. Average rainfall is more in northeast Chhattisgarh due to its hilly tract. • Chhattisgarh rainy season ranges from mid June to September. • Ambikapur, Bilaspur and Bastar experiences rain more than 100 cm. • Abujhmarh receives the maximum rainfall, roughly 187.5 cm.

question: The crops sown at the beginning of the first rains in July, during the south-west monsoons, are called.
explanation: The Kharif season varies by crop & region. However it starts at the earliest in May and ending the in January. Kharif crops are usually sown at the beginning of the first rains during the advent of the south-west mansoon season and they are harvested at the end of mansoon season (October-Novermber).

question: Which one of the following states receives the highest rainfall during winter months?
explanation: Tamil Nadu receives the highest rainfall during winter months. Tamil Nadu lies on the wind- ward side for these winds and hence gets more rainfall during the winter season. 367 / 896

question: What is the average rate of loss such that the volume of rainfall in excess of that rate will be equal to direct run-off?
explanation: Infiltration Index is defined as the average rate of water loss through infiltration, such that the volume of rainfall excess is equal to the value to direct run off. • Infiltration capacity (f) is the maximum rate at which a soil is capable of absorbing water in a given condition. • Run-off-coefficient (C) is a dimension less coefficient relating the amount of run off to the amount precipitation received.

question: The rainiest place in India is located in :
explanation: The rainiest place in India is located in Mawsynram of Khasi Hills in Meghalaya state. It is said to be the wettest place on earth, with an average annual rainfall of 11,872 mm. The village was recorded as the wettest place in the Guinness world record.

question: Consider the following statements : 1. The duration of the monsoon decreases from Southern India to Northern India 2. The amount of annual rainfall in northern plains of India decreases from East to West. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The amount of rainfall decreases from east to west owing to the progressive decrease in humidity of winds. Southern part of India, being near to sea, gets rainfall early for a longer duration than in northern part. Hence option 'c' is correct.

question: To which of the following are the origin and mechanism of the India monsoon related? 1. The circulation of upper air jet stream in the troposphere. 2. Differential heating of the Tibet Plateau and Ganga plain. 3. Differential heating and cooling of the huge landmass of Asia and the Indian Ocean. 4. Origin of Cyclones in the Bay of Bengal Select the correct answer from the codes given below : Codes :
explanation: Monsoon can be defined as seasonal reversal of Winds. Monsoon occupy 2 divisions, namely- The south-west Monsoon The Retreating Monsoon/ North East monsoon Monsoon is highly inconsistent phenomenon is dependent on following factors- • The differential heating and cooling of land & water. • The shifting of the position of Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ). • Movement of Westerly jet stream • Tropical Easterly Jet • Heating of Tibetan Plateau • Southern Oscillation (SO) • The presence of High Pressure area east of Madagascar.

question: Where did occur super cyclone on 29 Oct. 1999?
explanation: On October 29, 1999, a super cyclone with a wind speed of 260 kmph to 270 kmph had struck Odisha making it probably the greatest cyclonic disaster ever recorded in the last century.

question: Consider the following regions: 1. North-Eastern Madhya Pradesh 2. South-Western Andhra Pradesh 3. Saurashtra Which of the above regions is/are considered to be semi-arid
explanation: The semi arid region in the west of India includes the arid desert areas of Thar and Rajasthan extending to the Gulf of Kutch and cambay and the whole Kathiawar peninsula. Therefore, South-Western Andhra Pradesh and Saurashtra are considered to be semi-arid areas.

question: With reference to the climate of India, the 'Western Disturbances' originate over
explanation: A Western Disturbance is an extratropical storm originating in the Mediterranean region that brings sudden winter train to the northern part of the Indian subcontinent. The low pressure typically forms over the Mediterranean Sea and travels over Iran, Iraq, Afghanistan and Pakistan before entering India loaded with moisture.

question: Among the following, which one receives the highest amount of annual rainfall?
explanation: Among the following places the average annual rainfall is highest at Shillong. The average annual temperature in Shillong is 17.1°C. Precipitation here averages 3385 mm.

question: The place in India where minimum rainfall is recorded?
explanation: The place in India receiving the minimum rainfall is Leh. The average annual precipitation in these regions is less than 50 cms. Note:- • Mawsynram of Khasi Hills in Meghalaya, receives the highest rainfall in India. • Mawsynram is considered the wettest place and Jaisalmer is considered the driest place in India.

question: A line joining places with equal amount of rainfall is
explanation: • Isohyet is a line on a map connecting points having the same amount of rainfall in a given period. • Isohel is a line on a map connecting points having the same duration of sunshine. • Isoneph is a line on a map connecting points that the same average percentage of cloudiness. • Isonif is a line on a map connecting places of equal snow depth.

question: Which place in India receives lowest average rainfall?
explanation: Jaisalmer is place in India which receives the lowest rainfall. Jaisalmer is located in the centre of Thar Desert. It is also known as golden city and the largest city of Rajasthan. The average rainfall is 210 mm.

question: Annual range of temperature is maximum in the city of
explanation: Annual Range of temperature is defined as difference between mean temperature of hottest month and mean temperature of coldest month. In India New Delhi has maximum annual range of temperature and in World Ulaanbaatar, capital of Mongolia has maximum annual temperature range.

question: Rainfall intensity in Ganga plain decreases from

options.d: West to East Indian & World Geography

explanation: Rainfall intensity decreases from the East to West in Northern India because there is a decrease in the moisture of the winds. As the moisture bearing winds of the Bay Bengal branch of the South West monsoon move further, the moisture gradually decreases and results in low rainfall while moving westwards. Therefore, states like Gujarat and Rajasthan in Western India get very little rainfall.

question: Indian agriculture is a gamble in the monsoons, due to

options.c: Heavy rain fall on the Western Ghats
options.d: Winter rain fall in north-west India and Tamil Nadu

explanation: The Indian agriculture is considered gamble against monsoon because agricultural activities over almost all parts of India are very much dependent on the monsoon rainfall. In fact monsoon is the axis around which the Indian economy revolves. Hence, monsoon plays very important role in shaping Indian Economy.

question: The cause of winter rainfall in north-western India is :
explanation: The cause of winter rainfall in north-western India is western-disturbances. A western disturbance is an extra-tropical storm originate in the Mediterranean region that brings sudden winter rain to the north western parts of the Indian subcontinent. It is non-monsoonal precipitation pattern driven by the westerlies.

question: The eastern slopes of the Western Ghats have low rainfall because of
explanation: The eastern slopes of the Western Ghats have low rainfall because of their leeward location. As a result, these winds cause little rainfall in Eastern Ghats. The Western Ghats block rain bearing winds which cause rainfall on the western slopes. Whereas South West monsoon moves parallel to the eastern slopes, which cause less rainfall in the eastern slopes of Western Ghats because it is unable to block Moisture- Laden winds. The slope of Western Ghats called Sahyadri.

question: Tamil Nadu remains dry during the South- West monsoon period because :

options.a: it lies in the rain shadow region
options.b: Of the presence of Eastern Ghats

explanation: During the South-West monsoon Tamil Nadu remains dry during the South-West Monsoon, because it is located in rain shadow area. Tamil Nadu receives rain fall in the winter season due to North-East trade winds.

question: Which one of the following regions receives winter rainfall caused by western disturbances?
explanation: Punjab receives rainfall due to western disturbances. Western disturbances are extra tropical stroms that originate over Mediterranean and flows towards east. In India western disturbances bring winter rainfall in states like Punjab, Haryana and Western Uttar Pradesh and Snowfall in states like Himachal Pradesh, Jammu and Kashmir etc. (cid:1) India gets 5-10% of its total annual rainfall from western disturbances.

question: Forest 343. Which one among the following is the most widespread type of forest in India?
explanation: Tropical Dry Deciduous forest is the most widespread forests in India. They are also called the monsoon forest. They cover an area of around 341,100 square kilometers, extending across portions of Andhra Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Odisha and Telangana States. They spread over regions which receive rainfall between 70-200 cm. on the basis of the availability of water, these forests are further divided into moist and dry deciduous.

question: What type of forests cover the most area of India ?
explanation: Tropical deciduous forest covers the most area of India. They are also called monsoon forest. question: In disaster management, non-structural mitigation involves:
explanation: Non-structural mitigation in emergency management involves what people can do on a personal level that is not structurally or physically evident as protective defense such as a surge wall or a storm shelter and in this disaster management technique, we should educate and aware the community for social solution such as easy warning, evacuation planning and emergency response. Structural mitigation includes engineered solutions such as redesigning building and designing physical barriers for disaster events to reduce damage.
