question: Which of the following sequences of waves occur in an earthquake?
explanation: The sequence of Earthquake's waves is P( Primary) waves, secondary waves, surface waves. (i) Primary waves – This wave pass through every medium inside the Earth. Its average velocity is 8 km/s. This speed is greater than all the waves. Due to which these waves reach the surface first. (ii) Secondary waves – These are also called transverse waves. This wave passes only through solid medium. Its average velocity is 4 km/s. (iii) Surface wave – Surface waves travel only through solid medium. They are slower moving than body waves but are much larger and therefore more destructive.

question: The scientific study of earthquakes is Known as
explanation: • Seismology is the study of earthquakes and seismic waves that move through and around the earth. • Cosmology is the scientific study of the origin evolution and eventual fate of the universe. • Ecology is the study of the relationships between living organisms, including humans and their physical environment. • Pathology is a branch of medical science primarily concerning the cause, origin and nature of disease.

question: Which of the following earthquake waves are most destructive
explanation: Surface Waves are more dangerous than P Waves because they have greater amplitude and produce vertical and horizontal motion of the ground surface. The Slowest Waves, Surface Waves, arrive last. P wave is one of the two main types of elastic body waves, called seismic waves in seismology. P-Waves travel faster than other seismic waves and hence are the first signal from an earthquake to arrive at any affected location or at a seismograph. P-Waves may be transmitted through gases, liquids and solids. L Waves travel along the surface of the earth from the point directly above the quake or epicenter.

explanation: Archean Rocks – • These rocks have been termed as a result of the hot- molten earth becoming cold. These are the oldest and primary rocks. • The rocks of the Archean system are found mainly in Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Odisha, Chotanagpur plateau in Jharkhand and the southern-eastern part of Rajasthan.

question: The most suitable soil for the production of cotton is
explanation: Black Soil– • The most suitable soil for the production of cotton is Black Soil (Black cotton soil). • Black soil is also known as Regur Soil, Also suitable for Sugarcane, Jowar, Tobacco, Wheat and Rice. • It is rich in Iron, Magnesium and Aluminium but deficient in Nitrogen. • It is found in the Deccan area of Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh. Indian & World Geography

question: Study of soil in its natural setting is known in English as
explanation: Pedology is the study of soils in their natural environment, is important to understanding organic materials, minerals gases, liquids, and creatures make up soil. The people who study soils in these ways are pedologists.

question: In which state regur soil is found ?
explanation: Black soil is also known as regur soil. It is black in colour and ideal for growing cotton. This type of soil is typical of Deccan trap region spread over North-West Deccan plateau and is made up of lava flows. This soil is essentially found in the area of Maharashtra, Karnataka. (cid:1) Since it is moisture retentive and hiqh in iron, potash, lime calcium, magnesium, and humus, it is benefecial to agriculture.

question: What is khaddar?
explanation: They are Depositional soil- Transported and deposited by rivers, streams etc. New alluvium is termed as khadar and old alluvium is termed as bhangar. Black soil is also known as regur soil. It is good for cotton cultivation.

question: Which among the following soils is rich in minerals?
explanation: • Alluvial soils are formed mainly due to silt deposited by Indo-Gangetic-Brahmaputra rivers. In coastal regions some alluvial deposits are formed due to wave action. The Alluvial soil is highly rich in minerals. It is highly fertile and very good for agriculture. It often contains gravel, sand and silt. The chemical content of the soil will depend on where is located. • The parent material for most of the black soil are the volcanic rocks that were formed in the Deccan Plateau (Deccan and the Rajmahal trap). It is also called self-ploughing soil. The black soil is highly retentive of moisture. • Red soils along with its minor groups from the largest soil group of India. The main parent rocks are crystalline and metamorphic rocks like acid granites, gneisses and quartzited. 380 / 896 • Laterite soils mostly the end products of weathering. They are formed under conditions of high temperature and heavy rainfall with alternate wet and dry periods. Heavy rainfall promotes leaching (nutrients gets washed away by water) of soil where by lime and silica are leached away and a soil rich in oxides of iron and aluminum compounds is left behind.

question: Which of the following types of soils are mostly confined to river basins and coastal plains of India?
explanation: Alluvial Soils are mostly confined to river basins and coastal plains as river tends to erode the soils and deposit the highly fertile soil near river and its adjacent areas.

question: The older Alluvium of Indian plain is known as
explanation: Bhangar Plain are older alluvial plain which represents upland alluvial tract. These areas are well drained and make them suitable for cultivation. These area lies quiet above the flood limits of the neighbouring rivers. The soil is rich in humus. Khadar Plains – The new plain formed due to alluvial deposit’s along the course of the river. Enriched and formed by fresh deposits of silt every year. • Bangar Plain– The area of older alluvium of the Ganga Plain is known as Bangar plain. In the Gangetic plains, it has a low upland covered by laterite deposits and forms, the alluvial terrace of the flood plains. Bhabar Plain– Bhabar Plain is the gently-sloping coarse alluvial zone below the Shivalik Hills.

question: The best soil, for the growth of most plants, is
explanation: The best soil for most plants to ensure optimum growth is loam soil. This soil is an even mixture of all three types of soil i.e. clay, sand and silt. (cid:1) The estimated mixture is 40% sand, 40% silt and 20% of clay. (cid:1) Loam is just the right mixture of all three that it holds nutrients well, retains water but still drains property and allows oxygen to infiltrate.

question: Which of the following states has very little alluvial soil area?
explanation: Madhya Pradesh has very little Alluvial soil. Alluvial soils are soils deposited by surface water along flood plains, deltas. It is most fertile soil Indo- Gangetic plain is rich in Alluvial soil. Indian & World Geography

question: Which of the following is found in abundance in Red Soils
explanation: Iron is found in abundance in Red Soil which is the third largest soil group of India covering an area over the Peninsula from Tamil Nadu in the South to Bundelkhand in the North and Rajmahal hills in the East to Kutch in the West. Red Soil are generally found from the weathering of ancient Crystalline & Metamorphic rocks. Its Reddish colour is due to its high Iron content. Red Soil is also known as red Podzolic soil or laterite soil.

question: Terra Rossa is a type of
explanation: The term "Terra Rossa" comes from the Italian for "red soil" or red earth. Although Terra Rossa exists in other places in the world, these soil are common in area with Mediterranean climates alternating of a rainy and cool to warm dry season.

question: The soil found in the upland slope is known as :
explanation: Red Sandy soil mostly found in district of Dantewada, Kanker, Dhamtari and Durg. The soil crystal are fine sandy. Its fertility is low. Tikra is the local name of Red Sandy Soil (Plateau of Bastar). This soil is the second most common type of soil found in approximately 30% in the area of Chhattisgarh.

question: Extension of Red Loam soil in Chhattisgarh state is –
explanation: The red colour is mainly due to ferric oxides. Red loam soil is mixture of clay, sand, silt and organic matter. It is found in Dantewada, Bastar, Sukma and Bijapur regions of Chhattisgarh. Its pH value is upto 6.6% and suitable for cultivation of paddy and coarse gains.

question: Iron Oxide content in the rock forms which type of soil _____
explanation: Red soil is formed by a rock that contains iron oxide. It develops under warm, temperate and humid climates under deciduous or mixed forest. 381 / 896 • Black soil is mainly formed by weathering of lava rocks and have high iron, alumina and magnesia content. • Brown soil has a brown appearance caused by the coating of iron oxides weathered from the parent material. • Clay soil is a heavy soil that benefits from high nutrients. They can hold more water than most other soil types.

question: Identify the soil that covers large areas in Tamil Nadu, Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh and in formed as a result of the break-up of crystalline rocks into igneous and metamorphic and has a high concentration of iron oxide.
explanation: Red soil covers large areas in Tamil Nadu, Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh. Red soil develops on crystalline igneous rocks in areas of low rainfall in the eastern and southern part of Deccan Plateau. This soil develops a raddish colour due to a wide diffusion of iron in crystalline and metamorphic rocks. The fine-grained red and yellow soils are normally fertile, whereas coarse grained soils found in dry upland area are poor in fertility. Black Soils– are derivatives of trap lava and are spread mostly across interiors Gujarat, Maharashtra, Karnataka and Madhya Pradesh on the Deccan lava plateau and Malwa plateau. Forest Soils– are found in the hilly and mountainous areas where sufficient rainforests are available. It is found in northern plains beginning from Punjab West Bengal and Assam and also found in delta of different rivers in peninsular India. Alluvial Soils– are rich in humus as the deposited by three important river of Himalayas, Indus, Ganges and Brahmaputra River and also found in the eastern coastal plain of India, particularly in the river delta regions. 30.. Laterite Soil

question: Which of the following is NOT correct about Laterite soil?

options.a: This soil is poor in organic matter, nitrogen, phosphate.
options.b: this soil develops in areas with high temperature and high rainfall
options.c: This soil is widespread in the northern Plains and the river valleys.
options.d: This is the result of intense leaching due to tropical rains

explanation: In India, laterite soil is widespread, covering over 10% of the total geographical area, namely on the summits of the Western ghats, Eastern Ghats and southern parts of Maharashtra, Parts of Karnataka, West Bengal, Odisha, Jharkhand, Kerala, Assam and Meghalaya. Indian & World Geography • Laterite soil is reddish to yellow in color with a lower content of Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium, Lime and Magnesia with 90-100% of Iron, Aluminium and Titanium. • The laterite soil is formed under conditions of high temperature and heavy rainfall with alternate wet and dry period, which leads to leaching of soil, leaving only oxides of iron and aluminium.

question: Which one of the following is the most suitable plant for reclamation of alkaline soil?
explanation: Coconut is the most suitable plant for reclamation of alkaline soil. High alkalinity in the soil adversely affects the plant growth, thereby reduces the crop produces.

question: Which layer of soil contains humus that makes the soil fertile?
explanation: The humus makes the soil fertile and provides nutrients to growing plants. This layer is generally soft, porous and can retain more water. It is called the topsoil.

question: Which of the following is not a remedy measure of soil conservation?
explanation: The prevention of soil erosion is called as soil conservation and the ways can be as follows. Terrace farming is one way to do so and involves cutting terraces along a slope. These terraces reduce the speed of water flowing down the slope and help in soil conservation. Contour ploughing is also beneficial in reducing the flow of water down the slope and involves ploughing at right angle to the natural slope of land. Effective forming technique further help in soil erosion. Strip cropping can be used for soil conservation where strips of grass are allowed to stand between crops in large field. These strips of grass reduce the force of wind and thus prevent soil erosion.

question: Soft ground process is used for :

options.a: Transferring the texture from textural material

explanation: Soft ground, traditionally, is used to replicate the quality of a soft pencil line, to receive a textural transfer from various materials, or to accept the impression of various types of plant matter or animal skins. A soft ground, even when dry, remains soft and sensitive to pressure.

question: Melons grow best in
explanation: • Melons grow best in Sandy loam soils, with a pH between 6.0 and 6.5 • Rice, Wheat, Sugarcane grow in alluvial soils. • Cotton, Sugarcane, Tobacco, millets grown in Black soil. • Tea, Coffee, Rubber, Cinchona grown in laterite soils.

question: The largest multipurpose project in India is
explanation: Bhakra Nangal Project is the largest irrigation and the Major Multipurpose Project undertaking named after two dams Bhakra and Nangal on the Sutlej River at the border of Punjab and Himachal Pradesh. • The dam is 226 meters high and 518 meters in length with it greatest width at the base as 362 meters. • It is Joint venture of the states like Punjab, Haryana and Rajasthan intended to tackle the valuable water of the Sutlej to serve the concerned states. • The dam forms the Gobind Sagar Reservoir. • Bhakra Nangal dam is a concrete gravity dam with an installed capacity of 1,325 MW.

question: Nagarjuna Sagar dam is built on which river?
explanation: Nagarjuna Sagar Dam is built on Krishna river. The Krishna river originates in the western ghats near Mahabaleshwar. World's largest masonry Dam protected with 26 gates measuring 124.663m height. Nagarjuna Sagar Dam is located in Nalgonda district is built across river Krishna. The Dam has a storage capacity of nearly 11,472 million cubic meters with an irrigation capacity for 9.81 Lac areas of land. There are many dams constructed across the Krishna river–(i) Almatti Dam, (ii) Srisailam Dam, (iii) Nagarjun Sagar Dam. Indian & World Geography

question: Sardar Sarovar Dam is located on
explanation: Sardar Sarovar Dam (SSD) is build on the Indian Narmada River which is located in the village of Kevadia in the state of Gujarat. It is one of the largest and most controversial inter state, multipurpose river valley infrastructure development project in the country. • The foundation stone of the project was laid out by P.M Jawaharlal Nehru on 5 April 1961. • It is the largest dam to have been built under the Narmada valley project. • It has power facilities up to 200 MW and provides relief to drought from areas of Kutch and Saurashtra. Narmada River–The Narmada river is also called the reva and previously also known as Narbada. It is also known as life line of MP, for its huge contribution to the state of MP. • The Ganga is a trans-boundary river of Asia which flows through India and Bangladesh. It is said to be cultural glory of India. In Bangladesh, it is Known as "Padma". It originates from Gangotri glacier of Himalaya and merge into Bay of Bangal. Its total length is around 2525 km. The Ganga is seen as a mother to humanity. • Sutlej river– It is the longest of the 5 tributaries of the Indus river that gives the Punjab meaning "Five rivers" its name. It originates from Mount Kailash in Tibet. • Godavari river, river of central and south eastern India is sacred to Hindus. Its total length is about 1465km. It is second longest River after Ganga in India. It is also known as old Ganga of Indian peninsula.

question: On which river is built Hirakud Dam?
explanation: Hirakud Dam is located in the state of Orissa. It is the longest dam in India with a total length of 25.79 km. It is also in the list of the longest dams in the world. Hirakud Dam is situated on the river Mahanadi. • It is the longest dam in the world. • The Tehri Dam is the tallest dam in India on Bhagirathi River. Ganga– The Ganges or Ganga is a trans-boundary river of Asia which flows through India and Bangladesh. The 2,525 km. river rises in the Western Himalayas in the Indian state of Uttarakhand and flows South & East through Gangetic plain of North India into Bangladesh, where it merges into the Bay of Bengal. 383 / 896

question: 'Polavaram Irrigation Project' is being constructed on:
explanation: • Polavaram Project, is an under construction multi- purpose irrigation project on the Godavari River in Andhra Pradesh. The project has been accorded National project status by the Union Government of India. • On 7th January 2019, the Polavaram Project of Andhra Pradesh got its place in the Guinness Book of World Record for pouring 32315.5 cubic meters of concrete non-stop in 24 hours. • It will irrigate 38.18 lakh acres of land. It is also setup of 960 MW Hydro Power Plant, which will provide drinking water to 540 villages with a population of 28.5 Lac.

question: Ranjit Sagar Dam (Thein Dam) is a gigantic multipurpose river-valley project on which among the following rivers?
explanation: The Ranjit Sagar Dam, also known as Thein Dam, is part of a hydroelectric project constructed by the Punjab Irrigation Department, on the Ravi river on the border of two states of India, Jammu and Kashmir and Punjab. The project is largest hydroelectric dam of the state of Punjab. The dam is around and the equidistance 30 KM from both Pathankot in the Punjab and Kathua in J&K.

question: Which of the following projects is the first multipurpose river valley project of independent India?

question: Which one of the following is called the Mineral bowl of India’?
explanation: Chota Nagpur Plateau is known as the mineral bowl of India. It is also known as the storehouse of Indian minerals. The Chota Nagpur area has the most valuable concentration of mineral resources in India. The Damodar valley has vast coal reserves, and the Hazaribagh region is one of the main sources of mica in the world. Other minerals are copper, limestone, bauxite, iron ore, asbestos, and apatite (useful in the manufacture of phosphate fertilizers).

question: Despite having large reserves of coal, why does India import millions of tonnes of coal? 1. It is the policy of India to save its own coal reserves for future, and import it from other countries for the present use. 2. Most of the power plants in India are coal- based and they are not able to get sufficient supplies of coal from within the country. 3. Steel companies need large quantity of coking coal which has to be imported. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The imports are mainly to compensate the lack of good quality coal especially coking coal from the mining sources in the country. question: Which of the following is the width of the broad gauge used in Indian railway track?

(cid:1) The width of the standard gauge used in Indian Railway track is 1435 mm. (cid:1) The width of the metre gauge used in the Indian Railway track is 1000 mm. (cid:1) The width of the narrow gauge used in the Indian railway track is 762 mm and 610mm.

question: What is a hydrological drought?

Kaveri River rises on Brahmagiri Range near Coorg district, Karnataka flowing through the states of Karnataka and Tamil Nadu. question: Which of the following is a drought prone area?
explanation: In parts of India, failure of the mansoons causes water shortage, resulting in poor yields. This is particularly true of major drought-prone regions Indian & World Geography Southeastern Odisha, Maharashtra, Northern Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Gujarat, Telangana and Rajasthan. The Mahanadi delta's area in Odisha is a drought prone area. • Drought Prone Areas Programme (DPAP) is the "earliest area development programme" launched by the central government in 1973-1974 to tackle the special problems faced by those fragile area which are constantly affected by severe drought conditions. • For an area to be excluded from the drought-prone category, 30% or more of its-gross cropped area should be under irrigation.

question: A short duration crop in between two main season crops is termed as
explanation: Catch Crop–a quick-growing crop planted between two regular crops grown in consecutive seasons, or between two rows of regular crops in the same season. Catch crop are also crops that are sown to prevent minerals being flushed away from the soil. Cash Crop:- A cash crop or profit crop is an agricultural crop that is grown to sell for profit. It is typically purchased by parties separate from a farm. Intercrop:- Intercropping is the cultivation of two or more crops simultaneously on the same field.

question: During Earthquake the place which experience seismic event first is called as
explanation: The location below the Earth's surface where the earthquake starts is called the hypocenter & the location directly above it on the surface of the Earth is called the epicenter. • The Epicenter is the point where the greatest damage takes place, but the length of the subsurface fault rupture may indeed be a long one and damage can be spread on the surface across the entire rupture zone.

question: How many types of normal galaxy exist in the visible universe?
explanation: In 1936, Hubble debuted a way to classify galaxies, grouping them into four main type : spiral galaxies, barred spiral galaxies, elliptical galaxies, and irregular galaxies. More than two-third of all observed galaxies are spiral galaxies.

explanation: Mercury, the smallest planet in our Solar System and closest to the Sun is only slightly larger than Earth's Moon. Mercury is the fastest Planet zipping around the Sun every 88 Earth days.

question: ‘Nebular Hypothesis’ is a theory which is related to the origin of which of the following?
explanation: • The Nebular Hypothesis is model which explain the formation and evolution of the Solar System. It suggests that the Solar System formed from gas and dust orbiting the Sun. • This theory was propounded by Immanuel Kant. According to Kant’s Nebular Hypothesis, the solar system in its first state was a nebula, a hot, slowly rotating mass of rarefied matter, which gradually cooled and contracted, the rotation becoming more rapid, in turn giving the nebula a flattened, disklike shape.

question: The average length of a solar cycle is :
explanation: • The amount of magnetic flux that rises upto the Sun's surface varies with time in cycle called solar cycle. • The average length of solar cycle is 11 years. This cycle is sometimes referred to as the sunspot cycle. Sunspots are regions where the solar magnetic field is very strong. question: One Astronomical Unit (AU) represents the mean distance between:
explanation: An Astronomical Unit (AU) represents the mean distance between the Sun and the Earth. Its value is (approx)149,600,000 km (92,955,807 miles). Astronomers gives the Earth's changing distance throughout the year relative to the Astronomical Unit too.

question: Which celestial body is sometimes said to be made of cheese?
explanation: "The Moon is made of Green Cheese" was one of the most popular proverbs. Primarily made of the rock regolith, it is covered with impact craters which look similar to cheese holes but are actually from collisions with asteroids and other matter. (cid:1) The Sun – The Sun is the star at the center of the solar system. It is nearly perfect sphere of hot plasma, heated to incandescence by nuclear fusion reactions in its core, radiating the energy mainly as visible light and infrared radiation. It is by far the most important source of energy for life on Earth.

question: The location of sun on the first day of spring is called
explanation: The location of Sun on the first day of spring is called Vernal Equinox or March Equinox. This occurs twice each year around 20 March and 23 September. It is the moment at which the center of the visible Sun is exactly above the equator and day and night is equal length. (cid:1) In the Northern Hemisphere, the March equinox is called the vernal or spring equinox. While the September equinox is called the autumnal or fall equinox.

question: The sun is seen before the actual sunrise because of
explanation: The Sun is visible before actual sunrise and after actual sunset, because of atmospheric refraction. (cid:1) Atmospheric refraction is caused by the bending of light rays when they pass through the layers of the Earth's atmosphere by varying optical densities.

question: Abundant and unlimited energy on Sun is due to
explanation: Solar energy is a type of energy generated by the sun. Solar energy is created by nuclear fusion that takes place in the sun. Fusion occurs when protons of hydrogen atoms violently collide in the sun's core and fuse to create a helium atom. This process is known as proton-proton chain reaction, which emits an enormous amount of energy.

question: The scientist who first discovered that the earth revolves round the sun was
explanation: Nicolaus Copernicus was an astronomer and mathematician who was the first to discover that the Earth revolves around the Sun giving birth to the heliocentric model in which the Sun is at the center of the universe.

question: The source of Solar Wind is
explanation: The Solar Wind is created by the outward expansional plasma (a collection of charged particles) from the Sun's corona (outermost atomosphere). This plasma is continually heated to the point that the Sun's gravity can't hold it down. It then travels along the Sun's magnetic field lines that extend radially outward.

question: The phenomenon of "Midnight Sun" is caused by the

options.c: Rotation of the earth on its axis

explanation: The midnight sun, also known as the polar day, is a natural phenomenon seen with in the Arctic and Antarctic circles. The unique phenomenon is caused by the seasonal tilt of the Earth toward the sun during the Arctic or Antarctic summer.

question: Identify the nearest planet from the Sun.
explanation: Mercury is the nearest planet of the Sun and then Venus is the second nearest planet to the Sun and Earth is the third nearest planet to the Sun. The distance of Mercury to the Sun varies between 46 to 69.8 million km, from the Sun.

question: On the basis of the new definition of planet given by the International Astronomical Union (IAU) on August 24, 2006, which of the following is no longer a planet?
explanation: The International Astronomical Union (IAU) downgraded the Status of Pluto to that of a dwarf planet because it did not meet the three criteria the IAU uses to define a full sized planet.

question: Which of the following statements are correct regarding Exoplanet? 1. An international team of scientists received potential radio signals from Exoplanet. 2. This Exoplanet is 51 light years away from the earth. 3. This is possibly the first radio signal received from a planet beyond solar system.
explanation: An exoplanet or extrasolar planet is a planet outside the Solar System. An international team of scientists has collected the first possible radio signal from a planet beyond our solar system, emanating from an exoplanet system about 51 light years away.

question: Which of the following two planets lying between the Sun and the Earth?

question: The "Event Horizon" is an astronomical term related to
explanation: The 'event horizon' is the boundary defining the region of space around a black hole from which nothing (not even light) can escape. In other words, the escape velocity for an object within the event horizon exceeds the speed of light. In astrophysics an event horizon is a boundary beyond which events cannot affect an observer. The term was coined by Wolfgang Rindler in the 1950's.

question: Black Holes are celestial bodies which have strong
explanation: Black hole is a region in space where the pulling force of gravity is so strong that light is not able to escape. The strong gravity occurs because matter has been pressed into a tiny space. This compression can take place at the end of a star's life. Some black hole is the result of dying stars. Black hole's gravity can sometimes be strong enough to pull off the outer gases of the star and grow a disk around itself called the accretion disk. As gas from the accretion disk spirals into the black hole, the gas heats to very high temperatures and releases X-ray light in all directions.

question: A solar eclipse occurs when.....

options.a: the moon comes between the sun and earth
options.b: the earth comes between the sun and moon
options.c: the sun comes between the earth and moon
options.d: the sun, the moon and the earth are not in the same line

explanation: A solar eclipse occurs when the moon gets between Earth and the Sun, and the Moon casts a shadow over Earth. A total solar eclipse happens when the Moon completely covers the sun. It can take place only when the Moon is near Perigee, the point of the Moon's orbit closest to the earth. You can see a total solar eclipse only if you are in the path where the Moon casts its darkest shadow. A hybrid solar eclipse also known as an annular–total eclipse is the rarest type. It occurs when the same eclipse changes from an annular to a total solar eclipse and or vice versa along the eclipse's path.

question: The largest joint in human body is
explanation: The Knee joins the thigh with the leg and consists of two joints : one between the femur and tibia (tibiofemoral joint), and one between the femur and patella (Patellofemoral joint). It is the largest joint in the human body. The Knee is a modified hinge joint, which permits flexion and extension as well as slight internal and external rotation. The Knee is vulnerable to injury and to the development of osteoarthritis. The Knee joints is called “mobile” means allowing the bones to move.

question: The last great ice age was existed during the
explanation: The Pleistocene Epoch is typically defined as the time period that began about 2.6 million years ago and lasted until about 11, 700 years ago. • Pliocene : Spans from 5.332 million to 2.58 million year ago. • The Pliocene featured dramatic climatic change which ultimately led to modern species and plants. • The Triassic period from 252 million to 200 million year ago, saw the rise of reptiles and the first dinosaurs.

question: What is biomimicry?

options.a: A new movement in Sweden making fun of biology texts
options.b: Imitation of nature to create new products
options.c: A latest discipline in biological studies to study fatal diseases

explanation: Biomimetics or biomimicry is the emulation of the models, systems and elements of nature for the purpose of solving complex human problems. Biomimicry is a practice that learns from and mimics the strategies found in nature to solve human design challenges and find hope along the way.

question: The geographical concept of "Antipodal arrangement" refers to the

options.a: distribution of land diametrically opposite to sea
options.d: shifting of continents and oceans Indian & World Geography

explanation: The geographical concept of “Antipodal arrangement” refers to the distribution of land diametrically opposite to sea. It means that the continents and oceans are roughly arranged in such a way that land on one side of the globe is balanced by water on the opposite side of the globe. For example, the north polar Arctic ocean is opposite to the Antarctica Continent.

question: The term 'Isostasy' was coined by
explanation: Isostasy or Isostacy equilibrium is the state of gravitational equilibrium between Earth's crust (or lithosphere) and mantle such that the crust 'floats' over mental at an elevation that depends on its thickness and density. The general term 'Isostasy' was coined in 1882 by the American geologist Clarence Dutton.

question: Which one of the following is chemically formed sedimentary rocks?
explanation: Sedimentary rocks are the types of rock that are formed by the accumulation or deposition of mineral or organic particles at the Earth's surface. Sandstone is chemically formed sedimentary rocks. While gypsum, coal and limestone are found naturally.

question: The 'Coriolis effect' is produced by
explanation: When the Earth rotates, circulating air is deflected. Instead of circulating in a straight pattern, the air deflects toward the right in the Northern Hemisphere and toward the left in the Southern Hemisphere, resulting in curved paths. This deflection is called the coriolis effect. Earth Revolution :– Earth spins around its axis, just as a top spins around its spindle. This spinning movement is called Earth's rotation. At the same time that the Earth spins on its axis, it also orbits, or revolves around the Sun. This movement is called revolution.

question: The Tectonic Plates of the Earth lies in?

question: Approximatley what is the normal rate of temperature rise with depth from the surface of the Earth to the interior of the Earth?

The crust temperature is rising with depth due to the heat flow from the much latter mantle.

question: Which of the following rocks are oldest according to their geological structure :

question: Which of the following is a sedimentary rock?
explanation: Oolite is a sedimentary rock formed from Ooids, spherical grains composed of concentric layers. Sedimentary rocks are formed by lithification– consolidation and compaction of sediments. Granite is a common, coarse-grained, hard igneous rock consisting of quartz, orthoclass or microclines and mica. Anthracite is the highest rank coal. It is a black, shiny, hard rock and hot friable. Quartzite has high abrasion resistance and durability so it's a popular stone in the construction industry.

question: The term NIFE refers to

The core is made up of very heavy material mostly constituted by nickel (Ni) and Iron (Fe). The earth's layers are; SiAl (Silica + Aluminum) is the outer layer of continent. SiMa (Silica + Magnisium) is the mantle and oceanic crust.

question: Which of the following is used to denote heavy rocks of lithosphere?
explanation: SIMA– SIMA is an ancient compound term for the lower layer of Earth's crust. This layer is composed of rocks rich in magnesium silicate mineral, when the SIMA comes to the surface, it is basalt, so sometimes this layer is called the 'Ocean Layer' of the crust. Substratum- Ocean lithosphere consists mainly of mafic crust and ultramafic mantle and it is denser than continental lithosphere. Young oceanic lithosphere founded at mid ocean ridges are not thicker than the crust but oceanic lithosphere thickens as it edges and moves away from the mid ocean ridge. The lithosphere is solid part of the earth. It consists of three main layers crust, mantle and core. Lithosphere is the rigid outermost shell of a terrestrial type planet or natural satellite that defined by its rigid mechanical properties. It includes upper mantle and earth crust. SIAL– It is the name for the upper layer of earth crust. It composed of silicon and Aluminium. 447 / 896

question: Which of the following are studied by the petrologists?

(cid:1) Soil– Soil is a mixture of organic matter, minerals, gases, liquids and organisms that together support life. Earth's body of soil, called the pedosphere which has four important functions: (i) As a medium for plant growth. (ii) As a means of water storage, supply and purification. (iii) As a modifier of earth's atmosphere. (iv) As a habitat for organisms.

question: Which is the highest mountain peak of Africa ?

question: The rim of the Pacific is also called ring of ______.
explanation: The rim of the Pacific is also called the 'Ring of fire'. The Ring of fire, also to as the Circum - Pacific Belt, is a path along the Pacific Ocean characterized by active volcanoes and frequent earthquakes. Its length is approximately 40,000 Kilometers. The ring of fire is a direct result of movement, collision and destruction of plate tectonics under and around the Pacific Ocean. The world's highest active Volcano is Ojos del Salado (6,893m), which is in the Andes Mountains section of the Ring of Fire.

question: What is the name of the instrument used for detecting earthquake?

question: Which of the following is considered to be the largest volcano on Earth (in terms of its mass and footprint)?
explanation: Tamu Massif is an extinct submarine shield volcano located in the north western Pacific Ocean. The possibility of its nature as a single volcano was announced on 5 Sep. 2013, which of corroborated, would make Tamu Massif the largest known Volcano on earth. Tamu Massif was formed about 145 million years ago during the late Jurassic to easily Cretaceous period. Ojos del Salado is a dormant complex volcano in Andes on the Argentina-Chile border, the highest active volcano in the world. Mount Vesuvius is a somma-stratovolcano located on the Gulf of Naples in Campania, Italy. Mouna Kea is a dormant volcano on the island of Hawai.

question: Mount St. Helens is an active stratovolcano located in which of the following countries ?

question: Which of the following volcano is known as the ‘light-house of the Mediterranean’?

It is located on the Island of Lipari in the Tyrrhenian Sea, North of Sicily, Italy. (cid:1) It is one of the volcanoes of Calabrian Arc associated with the convergent plate boundary where African Plate is being subdued under Eurasian Plate. Fuji is an active volcano located on the Honshu Island of Japan.

question: In which part of India, the volcanic "Barren Island" is located?
explanation: Barren Island is a part of Indian Union Territory of Andaman and Nicobar Island, and lies about 138 km (86 miles) northeast of the territory's capital, Port Blair. It is the only confirmed active volcano in the Indian subcontinent and only active volcano along the chain of volcanoes from Sumatra to Myanmar.

question: What is caldera?
explanation: Caldera is a large volcanic crater, especially one formed by a major eruption leading to the collapse of the mouth of the volcano.

question: A place where an earthquake originates is called the ____.

question: Tsunami waves are NOT generated by:

question: How many seismic stations are required to locate the epicenter of an earthquake?
explanation: Three seismic staition are required to locate the epicenter of on earthquake. An earthquake is the shaking of the surface of the earth, resulting from the sudden release of energy in the earth's lithosphere that creates seismic waves such as P & S waves. The point where the energy is released is called the focus of an earthquake, alternatively, it is called the hypocentre. These waves travelling in different directions reach the surface. The point on the surface, nearest to the focus, is called epicentre. Earthquake waves are basically of two types–body primary waves and secondary waves. P & S waves are called body waves. Surface waves (L Wave) are the last to report on seismograph and are more destructive.

question: Who is known as the 'Father of the Modern Seismology'?

question: A river makes a very gentle sloping smooth wide valley

question: Which of the following layers of the atmosphere makes radio communication possible?

Each layer is characterized by a different gradient of the temperature as a function of altitude. Troposphere (0-15 kms) is the first layer above the Earth's surface. It is characterized by decreasing temperature with increasing altitude. Stratosphere (15-50 kms) is the second layer. In this layer the temperature rises as a function of increasing height. It's Ozone shield is preventing from harmful ultraviolet radiation of Sun to reach the Earth's surface. Mesosphere (50-90 kms) is the third layer where a lot of meteors burn up in this layer while entering the Earth's atmosphere. Thermosphere is the fourth layer. It includes the Ionosphere, where especially the auroras occur. Exosphere is the upper limit of Earth's atmosphere where the atmosphere merges into space.

question: The deflection of planetary wind is caused by the :
explanation: The Coriolis effect helps determine the direction of planetary or global, winds by causing them to curve or deflect, as the earth rotates. In the northern hemisphere, winds curve to the right in the direction of motion. Air moving toward the equator curves to the west, while air moving away from the equator curves towards the east. This pattern is reversed in the southern hemisphere where winds curves to the left in the direction of motion. The difference in temperature between land and sea also influences global winds.

question: What is the term for the strong wind that blows about 10 km above the earth?

question: Bauxite 1204. The largest producer of bauxite:

question: The largest proven crude oil reserve of the world lies in
explanation: Venezuela has the largest oil reserves of any country in the world, with more than 300 billion barrels of proven reserves. That is a 17.5% share of the entire global resource, and in 2011 the country surpassed Saudi Arabia to top worldwide list. Due to their viscous nature, Venezuela's Orinoco tar sands can be produced using conventional methods.

question: The leading producer of gold in the world is
explanation: China have got the first position for the production of gold in the world. The USGS estimates that China mined 455 metric tones of gold in 2016. Since gold began to be mined in the 1970s, gold production in China has rapidly increased. China finally overtook South Africa in 2007 as the world's top gold producer.

question: Pick out the correct statement

options.a: India receives 75 percent of its water supply from ground water mining
options.b: Saudi Arabia receives 75 percent of its water supply from ground water mining
options.c: Iran receives 75 percent of its water supply from ground water mining
options.d: Sudan receives 75 percent of its water supply from ground water mining

explanation: As much as 55% of India's total water supply comes from ground water resources. ⇒ Saudi Arabica receive 75% of its water supply from ground water mining. Indian & World Geography

question: A non-renewable resource is
explanation: A non-renewable resource is a natural substance that is not replenished with the speed at which it is consumed. Coal is a non-renewable energy resource. Some more non-renewable resources are crude oil, natural gas, and uranium. These are the resources that are processed into products that can be used commercially.

question: Which among the following countries has the world’s largest reserves of Uranium?
explanation: Australia has the world's largest reserves of Uranium. Australia possesses around 30% of the world's known recoverable Uranium reserves. In this ranking Kazakhstan has 2nd (22%) and Russia has 3rd (9%) largest Uranium reserves. Uranium reserves are reserves of recoverable Uranium, regardless of isotope based on a set market price.

question: The main focus of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy Management Centre is on :
explanation: Energy management is the means to controlling and reducing a building’s energy consumption. Sunlight is a renewable resource and its most direct use is achieved by capturing the sun's energy. Solar energy (power) is one of the cleanest and most abundant renewable resources. The Government of India has set a target of installing 175 GW renewable energy capacity by 2022.

question: Which country is the world leader in the number of solar power systems installed per capita
explanation: At the time of question Kenya was the world leader in the number of solar power system installed per capita but by early 2020 the leading country for solar power is China with 208 GW.

question: Which of the following is not a fossil fuel?
explanation: Fossil fuels are made of buried organic material. Examples of fossil fuels are Coal, Natural gas, Oil etc. Fossil fuels are made from decomposing plants and animals. These fuels are found in the Earth’s crust and contain carbon and hydrogen, which can be burned for energy. ⇒ Uranium is classified as a nuclear fuel not a fossil fuel. 469 / 896

question: The primary source of energy in the world is
explanation: The energy of the sun is the original source of most of the energy found on earth. We get heat energy from the sun, and sunlight can also be used to produce electricity from Solar (Photovoltaic) cells.

question: Orinoco oil belt is in –
explanation: The Orinoco Belt is a terriotory in the southern strip of the eastern Orinoco River Basin in Venezuela which overlies the world's largest deposits of petroleum.

question: Where is 'Ras Tanura' oil refinery located?
explanation: Ras Tanura, also spelled Ras Tanura, Arabic Ras–Al–Tannurah port on the Persian Gulf, in eastern Saudi Arabia, at the tip of a small peninsula. Developed by the Arabian American oil company after the discovery of nearby petroleum deposits in the 1930s, it is now a principal Persian Gulf terminal of the pipelines and has a modern port capable of accommodating the largest tankers. The town also has a refinery and storage tanks as well as hydroformers, producing high-octane gasoline. Aramco operates an industrial-training school and a hospital.

question: The largest automobile manufacturing unit in the world is located in
explanation: Ivanovo is known as Russian Manchester because of its textile manufacturing industry.

question: The magnetic levitation train of which country set a world record of 603 km/hr?
explanation: Maglev is a system of train transportation that uses two sets of magnets. The highest recorded maglev speed is 603 km/h, achieved in Japan by JR central's superconducting maglev on 21 April 2015, 28 km/h faster than the conventional TGV wheel rail speed record. Indian & World Geography

question: World's deepest metro station is in
explanation: Park Pobedy is the deepest metro station in Moscow (Russia), and one of the deepest in the world (after kyiv metro's Arsenalna). It is 84 meter (276 fit), undergrounds according to the official figures.

question: The world's largest airport 'Under One Roof' has officially opened in which city?
explanation: The world's largest airport ' Under One Roof' was opened on 29 October on the Republic Day of Turkey in Istanbul. It is dubled as phase-1A of the project – Istanbul New Air Port will have a capacity to handle 90 million passenger per year. The first phase cost 7.5 billion Euro.

question: Which is the first Airport in the world to operate fully with Solar Power?
explanation: Cochin International Airport, India's first airport built under a Public Private Partnership (PPP) model, has scripted another chapter in aviation history by becoming the first airport in the world that operates completely on solar power.

question: Norway to Alaska shortest air route is through
explanation: Norway is in Europe Continent and Alaska is in the North American Continent. The flight on the Arctic Ocean is the shortest route.

question: Which one of the following is the busiest ocean trade route?
explanation: The North Atlantic route is the busiest ocean trade. It connects the South America to North America (Panama canal), Africa to Europe and Europe to Asia (through Gibra Itar strait connecting Mediterranean sea. Suez canal :– The Suez canal is an artificial sea level waterway in Egypt, connecting the Mediterranean sea to the Red sea through the Isthmus of Suez. It is often considered to define the border between Africa and Asia. Constructed by the Suez canal company between 1859 and 1869, It was officially opened on 17 November 1869. 470 / 896

question: The World's busiest inland waterway is
explanation: The Largest and the busiest inland water ways in the world is the great Lake and the rivers St. Lawrence in USA. The Suez canal connects the Mediterranean sea to the Red Sea.

question: Which canal has reduced the distance from India to Europe by sea?
explanation: India is connected with Europe, North America and South America through both the routes the cape of Good Hope and the Suez canal in 1869, distance between India and Europe has been reduced by 7000 kms. (cid:1) The Suez canal is an artificial sea-level waterway running north to south across the isthmus of Suez in Egypt, to connect the Mediterranean sea and the red sea.

question: The cheapest option for the long distance freight movement is known to be
explanation: The cheapest option for the long distance freight movement is known to be waterways. The amount for loading and unloading goods is much cheaper if it has travel a long distance. If one has travel a short distance, movement of goods, human, or other from one place to another, among different modes of transport, railways are the cheapest.

question: The BTC pipeline which takes oil from Caspian Sea to the Mediterranean crosses which of the following countries?
explanation: Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan (BTC) pipeline is one of the great engineering endeavours of the new millennium. It runs 443 km through Azerbaijan, 249 km through Georgia and 1076 km through Turkey to the Ceyhan Marine Terminal.

question: Agriculture 1228. Primitive subsistence farming is also known as :
explanation: This type of farming is practiced on small patches of land. This is also called "slash and burn agriculture". A patch of land is cleared by slashing vegetation and then the slashed plants are burnt. It's basically a type of farming where farmer grow crops to provide shelter to their families and to feed them.

question: Which one of the following is not correct?

options.a: India is the largest producer of milk in the world
options.b: India is the second largest producer of sugarcane in the world.
options.c: India is the second largest producer of rice in the world.
options.d: India is the largest producer of wheat in the world.

explanation: India is not the largest producer of wheat in the world. China is the largest producer of wheat in the world. As of 2020 wheat production in China was 134,250 thousand tonnes that accounts for 20.66% of the world's wheat production.

question: Which country is the ‘bread basket’ of the world?
explanation: The USA is popularity known as the 'Bread basket' of the world because it supplies cereals, grains & rice to the whole world. • Argentina is famous for Pampas Grassland. • China is the world’s largest producer of rice.

question: Which country is considered as the ‘Sugar bowl’ of the world?
explanation: Cuba is referred to as the 'Sugar Bowl of the world'. It has the largest sugar Industry which is now decaying & its position is now taken up by Brazil. • Brazil is the largest sugar producing country followed by India. • The country of Cuba traces its roots in the production of sugar under the Spanish rule. It has the largest sugar industry in the world for which it is known as the Sugar bowl of the World.

question: Which country is the largest producer of wheat as per published by FAO for year 1994-2016?
explanation: China is the largest producer of wheat as per published by Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) for year 1994-2016. The latest forecast of world wheat production in 2020 (FAO) stands China first in the world.

question: Where is the headquarters of the beverage giant Coca Cola?
explanation: The Coco-Cola Company is an American multinational beverage corporation in corporated under Delaware's General Corporation Law and headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia USA.

question: ‘Current fallow land’ means

options.a: Land not cultivated in current year
options.b: Land not cultivated for the past two years
options.d: Land not cultivated for more than two years

explanation: Land not cultivated in current year is known as ‘Current Fallow Land’. • This is the land which is left without cultivation for one or less than one agricultural year. • Fallowing is a cultural practice adopted for giving the land rest. The land recoups the lost fertility through natural processes.

question: Largest producer of tea in the world is
explanation: • China is a largest producer of tea in the world with an annual production of around 2.4 million tonnes. • China has centuries-old tea production and consumption tradition. • India is the second largest producer of the tea, producing an average of 1,325,050 tonnes each year. • Assam is the largest tea-producing state of India, Approximately 52% of total tea produced in India comes from the state of Assam. • China, India, Sri Lanka, and Kenya produce 75% of the total tea of the world.

question: Which one of the following countries through well organized in agricultural, has mineral oil as the major source of foreign exchange earning?
explanation: Egypt is the country through well organized in agricultural has mineral oil as the major source of Foreign Exchange earned during years 1997-1998. Egypts economy relies mainly on agriculture, media, petroleum imports, natural gas and tourism.

question: Rearing of silk worms for the production of silk fibre is known as :
explanation: Sericulture is the science that deals with the production of silk by rearing of silkworms. Silk is called the queen of textiles due to its glittering luster, softness, elegance, durability properties and is discovered in China between 2600 and 2700 BC. Silk originating in the spittle of an insect is a natural fibrous substance and is obtained from pupae nests or cocoons spun by larvae known as silkworm. Indian & World Geography

question: Zhong Zhong and Hua Hua are the names given to which of the following cloned animal?
explanation: Two Baby macaque monkeys have become the first primate clones to be produced using the same approach that gave rise to Dolly the sheep in 1996. Big eyed, fuzzy-furred and spindly-limbed the macaques have been named Zhong-Zhong and Hua Hua. Tetra was the first cloning monkey. Zhen Liu created these monkey (Chinese scientist). Zhong-Zhong and Hua-Hua cloned monkey created by technique somatic cell Nuclear Transfer.

question: Breeding and management of bees is called
explanation: Apiculture – Breeding and management of bees are called apiculture. Sericulture – Sericulture is the process of cultivating silkworms and extracting silk from them. Silviculture – Silviculture is the method of regulating the growth, health, composition, and quality of forests to meet diverse needs and values. Pisciculture – The artificial reproduction, rearing and transplantation of fish is called pisciculture. It is also called fish farming.

question: Miscellaneous 1240. Which of the following conventional colours would you select to represent forested region on a topographic map?
explanation: Dark Green colour is used in the topographical map to show forest region. Topographic maps may use different colours to represent area features. Most topographic maps will use Green for vegetation or national parks and wildlife management area. They will also use Blue for rivers, lakes or other bodies of water. White colour is used in the topographical map to show rocky or bad lands and uncultivated lands.

question: Which of the following connects South America to North America?
explanation: The Isthmus of Panama in Panama links the the continents of North and South America and separates the Pacific and Atlantic Oceans. • North Amarica is the third largest continent in the world.

question: Which of the following was NOT a part of Gondwana Land?
explanation: The famous German scholar Alfred Wegener, in his Continental Drift Theory Stated that in the Carboniferous era all land masses the parts of the earth were intertwined. This vast Land was called Pangea, it was surrounded on all sides by an ocean called Panthalassa. Later pangea was divided into Gondwana Land and Angara land. South America, Africa, India, Madagascar Australia were part of Gondwana Land.

question: The Scale of the toposheet number E45C1 is
explanation: Topographic maps or topographic sheets or toposheets are detailed information of a land area, giving geographic positions and elevations for both natural and man-made features. Topographical maps, also known as general purpose maps, are drawn at relatively large scales. These maps show important natural and cultural featurs such as relief, vegetation, water bodies, cultivated land, settlements, and transportation network, etc. Each degree sheet has 16 toposheet of 1:50,000 scale and at the whole of the country is covered by 1:50,000 rigorous metric surveys in more that 5000 toposheets. Each 1:50,000 scale sheet contains four 1:25000 scale sheets. More than 35% of the country has also been covered on 1:25000

question: Which of the following Organization was released 'State of the Global Climate for 2020'?
explanation: World Meteorological Organization (WMO) released its annual State of the Global Climate for 2020 on April 20, 2021. (cid:1) According to the Report- Extreme weather combined with Covid-19 in a double blow for millions of people in 2020. However, the pandemic- related economic slowdown failed to put a brake on climate change drivers and accelerating impacts.

question: What framework was adopted at the third United Nations World conference on Disaster Risk Reduction, as an improved version of the Hyogo framework?
explanation: The International Tsunami Information Centre was established in 1965 by the Intergovernmental Oceanographic Commission (IOC) of the United Nations Educational scientific and Cultural Organization. The International Tsunami Information centre is located in Honolulu.

question: Which of the following is also called as devil- fish?
explanation: Octopus is known as "devil fish" due to its giant look and its hazardous behaviours, it is the harmful mollusks that can release toxins on feed other animals.

question: Hachures, the artificial lines drawn on maps, give an idea about the differences in the _____.
explanation: Hachures, the artificial lines drawn on maps, give an idea about the difference in the slope. • Hachures lines are drawn deeper, thicker and closer to show higher gradients and they are made lighter thiner and further apart to show slower (less steep) gradients.

question: Which of the following is NOT a global positioning system?
explanation: G.P.S.– It is the shortened form of NAVSTAR GPS .This is an acronym for Navigation System with Time and Ranging Global Positioning System. Global Positioning System :- • The satellite network system used to determine the exact geographical locations to be used by military or civil users is Called GPS. 473 / 896 • GPS is a system and it is made up of three parts : Satellites, Ground Station & Receivers • GPS used constellations of 24 Satellites to determine the accurate 3-D position of the user on the earth.

question: The maximum mass theoretically possible for a stable white dwarf star is known as:
explanation: The maximum mass theoretically possible for a stable white dwarf star is known as Chandrasekhar limit. Indian-American Astrophysicist Subramanian Chandrasekhar independently discovered this limit in 1930 at the age of 19, and made the calculation of this limit more accurate. Note:- The value of this limit is about 1.44 times that of a solar mass.

question: ‘Meadows’ are the grasslands of
explanation: Countries Grass land. South Africa Veld Argentina Pampas Central Asia Meadows Brazil Campos North America Prairies

question: Teesta water is shared by India and
explanation: Teesta River originates from Pauhunri Glacier (Chunthang, Sikkim) and is a tributary of the Brahmaputra River. •Teesta water sharing Dispute Negotiations are going on since 1983 between India and Bangladesh.

question: The Siachen glacier region, is a trouble spot between
explanation: The Siachen Glacier is located in the eastern Karakoram Range in the Himalayas near Ladakh. • Siachen Glacier is the longest glacier in the Karakoram region and second-longest in the world’s non-polar pass. • It is well known as the “Third pole of Earth”. • The Nubra River is originated from the Siachen Glacier. •It is trouble spot between India and Pakistan. •Operation Meghdoot (13 April 1984) was the code- name for the Indian Armed forces operation to capture the Siachen Glacier. Indian & World Geography

question: Among the following water bodies, which one has the highest salinity?
explanation: Salinity is defined as the ratio between the weight of the dissolved materials and the weight of the sample lake water. Salinity is expressed in the unit g/kg Very high salinity Bodies are– Lake Vanda-330%, Turkey. Great Salt lake-220%, Utah, USA. Red Sea-240%. Dead Sea-238%. Lake Vanda out of the given options has the highest salinity.

question: If a coastal area is affected by a high tide at 5 AM on Tuesday, it should normally experience next high tide at:
explanation: If a coastal area is affected by a high tide at 5 AM on Tuesday, it should normally experience next high tide at 5.25 PM Tuesday. Most coastal areas with some exceptions, experience two high tides and two low tides every lunar day.

question: Imaginary lines connecting places of equal rainfall are called
explanation: An Isohyet or isohyetal line is a line joining points of equal rainfall on a map in a given period. A map with isohyets is called an isohyetal map. Isobars – A line drawn on a weather map connecting points of equal pressure is called isobar. Isotherms – A line on a weather map that joins places that have the same temperature at a particular time. Isohalines are lines that join points of equal salinity in an aquatic system. Isohaline position refers to the distance of a near bottom isohaline from the mouth of a coastal water way.

question: The clouds which develop 'anvil' are
explanation: International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor (ITER) project is an international nuclear fusion research and engineering mega projects which is located in France. International Nuclear Fusion Research and Engineering mega projects aimed at replication the fusion processes of the sun to create energy on the earth.

question: Which of the following is known as 'Island of Pearls'?
explanation: Bahrain is the smallest country and the only island-state in the Persian Gulf and the wider middle East. It covers an area of 620 sq. km, about 3.5 times the size of Washington D.C. Bahrain consists of 33 island of which only 3 are inhabited.

question: 'El Nino' event is associated with
explanation: 'El Nino' and La Nina are complex weather patterns resulting from variations in ocean temperatures in the Equatorial Pacific region. EI Nino means "The little boy", or Christ child in Spanish. The term EI Nino refers to a large scale ocean atmosphere climate interaction linked to a periodic warming in sea surface temperatures across the central and east central equatorial pacific.

question: Which of the following is a typical feature of river erosion in youthful stage?
explanation: The features found in youthful stage of a river are all formed by the process of Erosion. The main features are–V-shaped valley, Gorges, Waterfalls, Potholes etc. The river erodes downwards in its youthful stage, called vertical erosion. The result of such erosion is a valley with steep sides and very narrow floors which looks like a "V". But when it meets with an obstacles of hard rock, being unable to cut them it flows around them and forms a Gorges like shape. • Ox-bow lake, Natural levees formed in old age stage while cut-banks are found in matured stage.

question: Which among the following is not the result of underground water action?
explanation: Water from the rainfall received on the Earth's surface seeps through the ground and accumulated under the ground surface is called ground water which gushes out in the form of springs. The land forms created by the actions of groundwater are sink hole, caves, stalactites and stalagmites, cave pillars etc. • Fiords are formed when the lower end of the trough is drowned by the sea and forms a deep steep-side inlet. These are formed mainly on Norwegian and South Chilean coast.

question: How much percentage of the world's water resources are available in India?
explanation: India accounts for 18% of the World population and about 4% of the world's water resources. One of the solutions to solve the country's water woes is to create Indian Rivers Inter-Link.

question: How many plates does the 'theory of Plate Tectonics' consist of?
explanation: Plates : Africa, Antartica, Eurasian, Indo- Australian, North American, Pacific and South American. • The Hawaiian Island was created by pacific plates, which is the world largest plates at 39,768,522 sq. miles.

question: What is an impermeable formation which contain water but are not capable of transmitting?
explanation: Aquicludes are the impermeable formations which contain water but are not capable of transmitting and supplying a significant quantity. • Aquifer : An aquifer is a saturated formation of earth material from which water is yield in sufficient quantity, due to the high permeability of earth material. • Aquifuge : Aquifuge is neither porous, not permeable, these are not inter-connected opening, so it does not contain water and does not allow it to pass through it.

question: If the earth's rotation is reversed what would be the IST when it is noon at Greenwich?
explanation: If the earth’s rotation is reversed and it is noon at Greenwich then IST will be 6:30 am. GMT [Greenwich Mean Time] It is the mean solar time at the Royal Observatory in Greenwich, London. IST [India Standard Time] It is the time zone observed throughout India, with a time offset of UTC+5:30 UTC [Coordinated Universal Time] It is the Primary time standard by which the world regulates clocks and time.

question: The geometric form of the earth is approximately that of
explanation: Oblate spheroid is rotationally symmetric ellipsoid having a polar axis shorter than the diameter of the equatorial circle whose plane bisects it. Oblate spheroid is the shape of the Earth and some other planets. It is like a sphere squashed from the top so the circumference around the equator. Shapes of this type are called ellipsoids.

question: Which one of the following is the largest producer of petroleum in South America?
explanation: Brazil accounts for oil production of about 2.5 million barrels per day and is the tenth-largest oil- producing country in the world. According to the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA), more than 90% of Brazils oil production is extracted from deep- water oil fields offshore. In addition, Brazil has nearly 13 billion of barrels in proven oil reserves, which is the second-largest in Latin America after Venezuela.

question: Consider the following statements : 1. Snowline occurs at a higher altitude in Eastern Himalayas than in the Western Himalayas. 2. Mangrove forests in India occur along the coastal areas of West Bengal and Odisha only. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Snowline occurs at a higher altitude in Eastern Himalayas than in the Western Himalayas. The Tidal or Mangrove forests grow by the side of the coast and on the edges of the deltas. Mangrove forests in India occur along the coastal areas of West Bengal, Odisha, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Gujarat and other state of India and also in region of fertile deltas of the Kaveri, Krishna, Mahanadi, Godavari and Ganga comprises of Mangrove forests. Hence statement 2 is incorrect. Note : In West Bengal, Mangrove forests are known as 'Sunderbans'.

question: Consider the following statements : 1. Earth receives the Sun's energy only at the infrared end of the spectrum. 2. Earth reradiates the Sun's heat only as ultraviolet energy. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Energy is transferred from the Sun to Earth via electromagnetic waves, or radiation. Most of the energy that passes through the upper atmosphere and reaches Earth's surface is in two forms; visible and infrared light. The majority of this light is in the visible spectrum.

question: Which currency and country are not matched among the following?
explanation: Country Currency U.S.A. – Dollar Australia – Australian Dollar South Korea – Won Saudi Arabia – Riyal United Kingdom – Pound

question: Litho stone is originally imported from :
explanation: Aloys Senefelder of Bavaria invented Lithography in 1798, but it was not used successfully for commercial prints in the United States until the mid-1820s. In the early days, the equipment needed for printing, had to be brought over from Europe. The Lithograph stones came from Rhineland (Germany) and also had to be imported.

question: Pre-historic site Catalhoyuk is located at :
explanation: Neolithic site of Catalhoyuk, which is located in the district of Cumra in Konya (Turkey) province, was discovered in 1958. The first site in the world where a city plan is depicted in wall painting, Catalhoyuk was inscribed on the world heritage list in 2012. Catalhoyuk existed from approximately 7500 BC to 6400 BC, and flourished around 7000 BC. In July 2012, it was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

question: The Erosional landforms of underground water are

options.b: U shaped valley, Hanging valley, Cirque

explanation: The Erosional landforms of underground water are Lapies, Poljes, Valleys and Caves.

question: Geographical cycle concept was postulated by
explanation: Geographical cycle, or cycle of erosion, theory of the evolution of landforms. In this theory, first set forth by William M. Davis between 1884 and 1934, landforms were assumed to change through time from youth to maturity to old age each stage having specific characteristics.

question: Which is the only land-locked country in South-East Asia?
explanation: Laos is the only land-locked country in South east Asia bordered by Cambodia, China, Myanmar, Thailand and Vietnam. Note– A landlocked country is a country that does not have territory connected to an Ocean or whose coastlines lie on endorheic basins. There are currently 44 landlocked countries. • Kazakhastan is the world’s largest landlocked country.

question: In the spring and autumn equinoxes (the period of equal duration of day and night), the position of the sun overheads at the equator corresponds with :
explanation: A Geyser is a spring characterized by an intermittent discharge of water ejected turbulently and accompanied by steam. Generally all Geyser field sites are located near active volcanic areas and the Geyser effect is due to the proximity of magma.

question: A Petroleum trap contains the constituents in ascending order of :
explanation: A Petroleum trap contains the constituents in ascending order of water, oil and gas. In Petroleum geology, a trap is a geological structure affecting the reservoir rock and cap rock of a Petroleum system allowing the accumulation of hydrocarbons in a reservoir.

question: A dome-shaped snow houses 'igloo' is the home of
explanation: An igloo also known as a snow house or snow hut, is a type of shelter. Igloos are often associated with all in unit and Eskimos peoples. They were traditionally used only by the people of Canada's Central Arctic and Greenland's Thule area.

question: In which year did man first land on the moon?
explanation: On July 16th July 1969, Neil Armstrong became the first human to step on the moon. He and Aldrin walked around for three hours.

question: The primary source of energy in the world is
explanation: The energy of the sun is the original source of most of the energy found on earth. We get solar heat energy from the sun, and sunlight can also be used to produce electricity from Solar (Photovoltaic) cells.

question: The two currently favoured categories of sites for dumping of radio-active wastes are 1. Deep mined cavities in stable ecological formation of land 2. The desert regions 3. 50 to 100 metres below the seabed away from tectonic plate margins
explanation: Radioactive waste material includes all those substances which are either Radioactive in themselves are contaminated by Radioactivity and which should not be considered useful for the future and the environment. These include radioactive elements like Uranium, Thorium, Radon, Carbon and Radium. • In general, Radioactive waste is separated into three categories. Which depending on its level of Radioactivity and the length of time is remains hazardous. (i) Low-Level Waste (LLW) (ii) Intermediate Level Waste (ILW) (iii) High Level Waste (HLW) • Dumping (Disposal) of radioactive waste (i) Deep mined cavities in stable ecological formation of land. (ii) Disposal in the desert regions.

options.b: It is a famous castle in Romania.

question: Which of the following is the longest river?
explanation: Nile River is considered of be the longest River in the world with a length of 6650 meters. It has two major tributaries, first is the white Nile which originates from Jinja, Lake Victoria & Second is the blue Nile that begins of Lake Tana, in Ethiopia. Indian & World Geography

question: Which one of the following tribes treats their cattle with great respect and affection and do not kill them for food or for sale as meat?
explanation: The Maasai are a Nilotic ethnic group inhabiting Northern, Central and Southern Kenya and Northern Tanzania. The Maasai speak the Maa language, a member of the Nilotic language family that is related to the Dinka, Kalenjin and Nuer languages Massai people speak the official languages of Kenya and Tanzania, Swahili and English. The Massai heavily depend on cattle for nutrition Maasai diet consists of six basic food milk, meats, raw blood, honey and tree bark. Most of the milk is consumed as fermented milk or buttermilk. Milk consumption figures are very high by any standards.

question: Which one of the following is located in the region with more than 150 cm. average annual rainfall?

question: The study of caves is called
explanation: The study of caves is called speleology. The word speleology comes from the Latin word "speleum" and the Greek word "spelaion", both of which mean "cave". The ending "ology", means "the study of." (cid:1) Oceanology– Oceanology is the study of the physical and biological aspects of the ocean.

question: Hydrology is the study of science of what?
explanation: Hydrology is the scientific study of the movement, distribution and management of water on earth and other planets, including the water cycle, water resources and environmental watershed sustainability. A practitioner of hydrology is called a hydrologist. 478 / 896

question: What is the SI unit of mass?
explanation: The S.I Unit was accepted in 1960. In fact, this method is a modified of MKS (Metre Kilogram Second). It has 7 fundamental units and 2 supplementary units, length, mass, time, electric current, temperature, light-intensity, amount of matter are the basic units and radians and steradian come in the category of complementary units, it is noteworthy that the S.I unit of mass is kilogram.

question: What is the SI Unit of quantity of heat?
explanation: Generally in the SI system all forms of energy are measured in term of Joules. question: The time taken by light to travel from Sun to Earth is equal to____.
explanation: 1 Astronomical unit (AU) represents the mean distance between Earth and Sun. An AU is approximately 93 million miles (150 million kilometers) or 8 light minutes. Note:- The average distance between the Earth and the Sun is 149599790.21 km. The speed of light as we 3×108 know is m/s. Hence the time taken by light to reach earth from sun is 149599790.21×1000m = 3×108m/s = 498.665 sec = 8 minutes (approx)

question: One Astronomical Unit (AU) is approximately equal to ----------- .
explanation: The Astronomical Unit is a unit of length, roughly the distance from Earth to the Sun. 1AU=150millionkm

question: What is measured in Cusec?

question: The ratio between mile and kilometer is
explanation: The distance in km is equal to the distance in miles multiplied by 1.609344. Since one mile is equal to 1.609344 km. So, required ratio = 1 : 1.6 Miles and kilometer both units are used to measure distance or length. Mile– One mile is also equal to 5280 feet or 1760 yards. Kilometer– One kilometer is equal to 1000 meters. Science

question: The unit of Sound is
explanation: We measure sound intensity (also referred to as sound power or sound pressure) in units called decibels. Decibels (dB) are named in honor of Alexander Graham Bell, the inventor of both the Telephone and the Audiometer.

question: The unit used to describe weight of diamond is
explanation: Carat is the unit of measurement for the physical weight of diamonds. One carat equals to 1 0.200 grams or gram and is sub divided into 100 5 points. For comparison, in units more familiar in the United State's, one carat equals to 0.007 ounce. 1 carat diamond is approximately 6.5 mm diameter. This is the size of a round diamond cut to ideal proportions. • Mound– A varying unit of weight in some Asian countries especially an Indian unit of weight equivalent to about 37 kg. • Tola is a traditional ancient Indian and south Asian unit of mass, now standardized as 180 troy grains (11.6638038 grams). • Milligram is the basic unit of mass in metric system.

question: 'Parsec' is a unit of measurement of ______.
explanation: The Parsec (symbol: Pc) is a unit of length used to measure the large distances to astronomical objects outside the Solar System. Parsec is a unit for expressing astronomical distance. 1 Parsec = 3.086 × 106 m

question: In science, a push or a pull on an object is called as ........
explanation: In science a push or a pull on an object is called as force. A force is any interaction that, when unopposed, will change the motion of an object. A force can cause on object with mass to change its velocity (which includes to begin moving from a state of rest). A force has both magnitude and direction, making it a vector quantity. The SI unit of force is 'Newton (N)' and represented by the symbol 'F'.

question: ______ is correctly represents one Parsec.
explanation: The Parsec is a unit of length used to measure the large distances to astronomical objects outside the solar system, approximately equal to 3.261 light years or 206.000 astronomical units. i.e. 30.9 trillion km.

question: Surface tension has the units
explanation: The force per unit length perpendicular to a line drawn in the surface of the liquid is the surface tension. SurfaceTension(σ)=F/ℓ Its SI unit is Newton's per meter and its CGS unit is dynes per centimeter.

question: An instrument, that is used for the detection of earthquake, is
explanation: A Seismograph or Seismometer is an instrument used to detect and record earthquakes. Generally it consists of a mass attached to fixed base. Lactometer is an instrument used to measure the density of milk. Holograph- A manuscript or other document written completely in the hand writing of the person above whose name it appears. Science

question: Wet sand is more compact than dry sand

options.a: Because water creates water bridges between sand grains
options.b: Because sand acquires the strength of concrete
options.d: Because the volume of sand decreases by half

explanation: At equal volumes of wet and dry sand would not weigh the same, the wet sand would weight more and also compact more because it has more mass, because water creates water bridges between sand grains and the mass of the water.
